modules.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin
documentation:
This module provides the NetlistOpsMixin class.

Copyright 2023 Michael Hayes, UCECE

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.admittance
documentation:
Return driving-point Laplace-domain admittance between nodes
Np and Nm with independent sources killed and initial
conditions ignored.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.conductance
documentation:
Return conductance (inverse resistance) between nodes Np and Nm
with independent sources killed.  The result is in the AC (omega)
domain.    See also resistance, reactance, susceptance.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.current_gain
documentation:
Create Laplace-domain current transfer function I2(s) / I1(s) where:
I1 is the test current applied between N1p and N1m
I2 is the measured short-circuit current flowing from N2m to N2p

Note, the currents are considered to be flowing into the
positive nodes as is the convention with two-ports.  Thus the
input and output currents have opposite directions and so a
piece of wire has a current gain of -1.

Note, independent sources are killed and initial conditions
are ignored.

Alternative forms are:
    current_gain(N1p, N1m, N2p, N2m)
    current_gain(cpt1, cpt2)
    current_gain((N1p, N1m), cpt2)
    current_gain(cpt1, (N2p, N2m))

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.impedance
documentation:
Return driving-point Laplace-domain impedance between nodes
Np and Nm with independent sources killed and initial
conditions ignored.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.Isc
documentation:
Return short-circuit transform-domain current between nodes Np and
Nm.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.isc
documentation: Return short-circuit t-domain current between nodes Np and Nm.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.ladder
documentation:
Return two-port unbalanced ladder network or `None` if the netlist
does not have a ladder topology between the specified nodes.

The input port is defined by the nodes `N1p` and `N1m`.
The output port is defined by the nodes `N2p` and `N2m`.

The nodes `N1p` and `N1m` must be the same.

Alternative forms are:
    ladder(N1p, N1m, N2p, N2m)
    ladder(cpt1, cpt2)
    ladder((N1p, N1m), cpt2)
    ladder(cpt1, (N2p, N2m))

This method can be used to generate a transfer function, for example:

`cct.ladder(1, 0, 10, 0).voltage_gain`

This can be much faster than using `cct.voltage_gain(1, 0, 10, 0)`
for circuits with a large ladder topology, since the latter method needs
to determine all the node voltages.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.norton
documentation:
Return Laplace-domain Norton model between nodes Np and Nm.

If Np is a component name, create model using its component nodes.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.oneport
documentation:
Return oneport object between nodes Np and Nm.  This might be a
Thevenin network, a Norton network, or a single component.

If Np is a component name, create model using its component nodes.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.reactance
documentation:
Return reactance between nodes Np and Nm with independent
sources killed.  The result is in the AC (omega) domain.
See also conductance, resistance, susceptance.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.resistance
documentation:
Return resistance between nodes Np and Nm with independent
sources killed.  The result is in the AC (omega) domain.
See also conductance, reactance, susceptance.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.state_space
documentation:
Generate state-space representation.

`node_voltages` is a list of node names to use as voltage outputs.
If `None` use all the unique node names.

`branch_currents` is a list of component names to use as
current outputs.  If `None` use all the components.

Here's an example:
`cct = Circuit('cct.sch')
ss = cct.state_space(node_voltages=['1', '3'], branch_currents=['L1', 'L2'])`

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.susceptance
documentation:
Return susceptance (inverse reactance) between nodes Np and Nm with
independent sources killed.  The result is in the AC (omega)
domain.  See also conductance, reactance, resistance.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.thevenin
documentation:
Return Laplace-domain Thevenin oneport model between nodes Np and Nm.

If Np is a component name, create model using its component nodes.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.transfer
documentation:
Create Laplace-domain voltage transfer function V2(s) / V1(s) where:
V1 is V[N1p] - V[N1m]
V2 is V[N2p] - V[N2m]

Note, independent sources are killed and initial conditions
are ignored.

Alternative forms are:
    transfer(N1p, N1m, N2p, N2m)
    transfer(cpt1, cpt2)
    transfer((N1p, N1m), cpt2)
    transfer(cpt1, (N2p, N2m))

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.twoport
documentation:
Create Laplace-domain twoport model for two-port defined by nodes N1p, N1m, N2p, and N2m, where:
I1 is the current flowing into N1p and out of N1m
I2 is the current flowing into N2p and out of N2m
V1 is V[N1p] - V[N1m]
V2 is V[N2p] - V[N2m]

`model` is `A, `B`, `G`, `H`, `Y`, or `Z`.

Alternative forms are:
    twoport(N1p, N1m, N2p, N2m)
    twoport(cpt1, cpt2)
    twoport((N1p, N1m), cpt2)
    twoport(cpt1, (N2p, N2m))

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.transadmittance
documentation:
Create Laplace-domain transadmittance (transfer admittance) function
I2(s) / V1(s) where:
  V1 is the test voltage applied between N1p and N1m
  I2 is the measured short-circuit current flowing from N2m to N2p.

Note, I2 is considered to be flowing into the positive node as
is the convention with two-ports.  Thus the transadmittance of
a series resistor with resistance R is -1 / R.

Note, independent sources are killed and initial conditions
are ignored.

Alternative forms are:
    transadmittance(N1p, N1m, N2p, N2m)
    transadmittance(cpt1, cpt2)
    transadmittance((N1p, N1m), cpt2)
    transadmittance(cpt1, (N2p, N2m))

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.transimpedance
documentation:
Create Laplace-domain transimpedance (transfer impedance) function
V2(s) / I1(s) where:
  I1 is the test current applied between N1p and N1m
  V2 is the measured open-circuit voltage between N2p and N2m.

Note, I1 is considered to be flowing into the positive node as
is the convention with two-ports.

Note, independent sources are killed and initial conditions
are ignored.

Alternative forms are:
    transimpedance(N1p, N1m, N2p, N2m)
    transimpedance(cpt1, cpt2)
    transimpedance((N1p, N1m), cpt2)
    transimpedance(cpt1, (N2p, N2m))

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.Voc
documentation:
Return open-circuit transform-domain voltage between nodes Np and
Nm.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.voc
documentation: Return open-circuit t-domain voltage between nodes Np and Nm.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistopsmixin.NetlistOpsMixin.voltage_gain
documentation:
Create Laplace-domain voltage transfer function V2(s) / V1(s) where:
V1 is the test voltage applied between N1p and N1m
V2 is the measured open-circuit voltage between N2p and N2m

Note, independent sources are killed and initial conditions
are ignored.

Alternative forms are:

documentation:
This module provides the NormFourierDomainExpression class
to represent F-domain (normalized  Fourier domain)
expressions.

Copyright 2021--2024 Michael Hayes, UCECE

Create NormFourierDomainExpression object.
If `arg` is Fsym return F

documentation:
Convert to discrete-time domain using inverse discrete-time
Fourier transform.

methods.name: lcapy.normfexpr.NormFourierDomainExpression.fourier

methods.name: lcapy.normfexpr.NormFourierDomainExpression.angular_fourier

documentation: Convert to normalized Fourier domain.

methods.name: lcapy.normfexpr.NormFourierDomainExpression.phasor
documentation: Convert to phasor domain.

methods.name: lcapy.normfexpr.NormFourierDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response at values specified by `Fvector`.

If `Fvector` is a tuple, this sets the frequency limits.

`plot_type` - 'dB-phase', 'dB-phase-degrees', 'mag-phase',
'mag-phase-degrees', 'real-imag', 'mag', 'phase',
'phase-degrees', 'real', or 'imag'.

The default `plot_type` for complex data is `dB-phase`.

`kwargs` include:
`axes` - the plot axes to use otherwise a new figure is created
`xlabel` - the x-axis label
`ylabel` - the y-axis label
`ylabel2` - the second y-axis label if needed, say for mag and phase
`xscale` - the x-axis scaling, say for plotting as ms
`yscale` - the y-axis scaling, say for plotting mV
`norm` - use normalized frequency
`dbmin` - the smallest value to plot in dB (default -120)
in addition to those supported by the matplotlib plot command.

The plot axes are returned.  This is a tuple for magnitude/phase or
real/imaginary plots.

There are many plotting options, see lcapy.plot and
matplotlib.pyplot.plot.

For example:
    V.plot(Fvector, log_frequency=True)
    V.real.plot(Fvector, color='black')
    V.phase.plot(Fvector, color='black', linestyle='--')

By default complex data is plotted as separate plots of
magnitude (dB) and phase.

methods.name: lcapy.normfexpr.NormFourierDomainExpression.bode_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Bode
plot (but without the straight line approximations).  fvector
specifies the normalised frequencies.  If it is a tuple (f1,
f2), it sets the frequency limits.  Since a logarithmic
frequency scale is used, f1 must be greater than 0.

`unwrap` controls phase unwrapping (default True).

For more info, see `plot`.

methods.name: lcapy.normfexpr.NormFourierDomainExpression.nyquist_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response as a Nyquist plot (imaginary part versus
real part).  fvector specifies the normalised
frequencies.  If it is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency
limits as (f1, f2).

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

The unit circle is shown by default.  This can be disabled with `unitcircle=False`.

methods.name: lcapy.normfexpr.NormFourierDomainExpression.nichols_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response as a Nichols plot (dB versus phase).
fvector specifies the normalised frequencies.  If it is a
tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits as (f1, f2).

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.
Normalized  Fourier domain expression or symbol.

modules.name: lcapy.symbolregistry
documentation:
This module maintains a registry of symbols.

Copyright 2022--2023 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.symbolregistry.SymbolRegistry

methods.name: lcapy.symbolregistry.SymbolRegistry.add_from_expr
documentation: Add all the symbols in an expression.

methods.name: lcapy.symbolregistry.SymbolRegistry.delete_user
documentation: Delete user symbols.

methods.name: lcapy.symbolregistry.SymbolRegistry.user
documentation: Return user defined symbols.

methods.name: lcapy.symbolregistry.SymbolRegistry.expr
documentation: Return expr defined symbols.

methods.name: lcapy.symbolregistry.SymbolRegistry.delete_expr
documentation: Delete expr symbols.

methods.name: lcapy.symbolregistry.SymbolRegistry.kind
documentation: Return the symbol kind or 'unknown' if unknown.

This maintains a registry of symbols so that there is only
a single symbol of a given name.

The symbol kinds are:
'domain' defined explicitly by Lcapy for domain variables
'misc' defined explicitly by Lcapy for misc. variables, such as f0
'user' defined explicitly by the user using `symbol()` or `symbols()`
'expr' defined implicitly by the user using `expr()`.

Note `expr` symbols do not overwrite symbols of the same name that
are defined explicitly.

modules.name: lcapy
documentation:
Lcapy is a Python library for symbolic linear circuit and signal analysis.

Lcapy can analyse circuits described with netlists using modified nodal
analysis.  See lcapy.netlist

Alternatively, Lcapy can analyse networks and circuits formed by
combining one, two, and three port networks.  See lcapy.oneport

For detailed documentation see http://lcapy.readthedocs.io/en/latest

Copyright 2014--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.show_version
documentation: Show versions of Lcapy, SymPy, NumPy, MatplotLib, SciPy, and Python.
Do not import units.u since this will conflict with unit step

modules.name: lcapy.noiseexpr
documentation:
This module provides the NoiseExpression class to represent noise expressions.

Copyright 2020--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.noiseexpr.NoiseExpression

methods.name: lcapy.noiseexpr.NoiseExpression.__add__
documentation: Add noise spectra (on power basis if uncorrelated).

methods.name: lcapy.noiseexpr.NoiseExpression.rms
documentation: Calculate rms value.

methods.name: lcapy.noiseexpr.NoiseExpression.sample
documentation:
Return a sample function (realisation) of the noise process
evaluated at time values specified by vector t.   If t is an integer,
this returns `t` samples.

methods.name: lcapy.noiseexpr.NoiseExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response at values specified by fvector.

There are many plotting options, see matplotlib.pyplot.plot.

For example:
    V.plot(fvector, log_frequency=True)
    V.real.plot(fvector, color='black')
    V.phase.plot(fvector, color='black', linestyle='--')

By default complex data is plotted as separate plots of magnitude (dB)
and phase.

Frequency domain (one-sided) noise spectrum expression (amplitude
spectral density).

This characterises a zero-mean Gaussian noise process.

When performing arithmetic on two noiseExpr expressions it is
assumed that they are uncorrelated unless they have the same nid
(noise indentifier).  If the nid is not specified, a new one is
created.

Uncorrelated noise expressions are added in quadrature (on a power
basis).  Thus (NoiseExpression(3) + NoiseExpression(4)).expr = 5
since 5 = sqrt(3**2 + 4**2)

NoiseExpression(3) != NoiseExpression(3) since they are different
noise realisations albeit with the same properties.  However,
NoiseExpression(3).expr == NoiseExpression(3).expr.  Similarly,
NoiseExpression(3, nid='n1') == NoiseExpression(3, nid='n1') since
they have the same noise identifier and thus have the same
realisation.

Caution: The sum of two noise expressions generates a noise
expression with a new nid.  This can lead to unexpected results
since noise expressions with different nids are assumed to be
uncorrelated.  For example, consider:
a = NoiseExpression(3);
b = NoiseExpression(4)
a + b - b gives sqrt(41) and a + b - a gives sqrt(34).

This case is correctly handled by the Super class since each noise
component is stored and considered separately.

(Voltage(a) + Voltage(b) - Voltage(b)).n gives 3 as expected.

modules.name: lcapy.inverse_laplace
documentation:
This module provides support for the unilateral inverse Laplace
transform.

Copyright 2021--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.inverse_laplace.inverse_laplace_transform
documentation:
Calculate inverse Laplace of X(s) and return x(t).

The unilateral Laplace transform cannot determine x(t) for n < 0
unless given additional information:

`dc` -- x(t) = constant
`causal` -- x(t) = 0 for n < 0.
`ac` -- x(t) = A cos(a * n) + B * sin(b * n)

functions.name: lcapy.inverse_laplace.ILT
documentation:
Calculate inverse Laplace of X(s) and return x(t).

The unilateral Laplace transform cannot determine x(t) for n < 0
unless given additional information:

`dc` -- x(t) = constant
`causal` -- x(t) = 0 for n < 0.
`ac` -- x(t) = A cos(a * n) + B * sin(b * n)

methods.name: lcapy.inverse_laplace.InverseLaplaceTransformer.tline_end
documentation:
Attempt to find response at end of unterminated lossless
transmission line.

methods.name: lcapy.inverse_laplace.InverseLaplaceTransformer.tline_start
documentation:
Attempt to find response at start of unterminated lossless
transmission line.

Copyright 2022--2024 Michael Hayes, UCECE

documentation: Attempt inverse Fourier transform.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.time
documentation: Alias for inverse_fourier.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression._fourier
documentation: Convert to  Fourier domain.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.fourier
documentation: Convert to Fourier domain.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.norm_fourier
documentation: Convert to normalized Fourier domain.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.norm_angular_fourier
documentation: Convert to normalized  Fourier domain.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.laplace
documentation: Convert to Laplace domain.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.phasor
documentation: Convert to phasor domain.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot  frequency response at values specified by vvector.

There are many plotting options, see matplotlib.pyplot.plot.

For example:
    V.plot(fvector, log_frequency=True)
    V.real.plot(fvector, color='black')
    V.phase.plot(fvector, color='black', linestyle='--')

By default complex data is plotted as separate plots of magnitude (dB)
and phase.

`kwargs` include:
`axes` - the plot axes to use otherwise a new figure is created
`xlabel` - the x-axis label
`ylabel` - the y-axis label
`ylabel2` - the second y-axis label if needed, say for mag and phase
`xscale` - the x-axis scaling, say for plotting as ms
`yscale` - the y-axis scaling, say for plotting mV
`norm` - use normalized frequency
`dbmin` - the smallest value to plot in dB (default -120)
in addition to those supported by the matplotlib plot command.

The plot axes are returned.  This is a tuple for magnitude/phase or
real/imaginary plots.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.bode_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Bode
plot (but without the straight line approximations).  vvector
specifies the  frequencies.  If it is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets
the frequency limits.   Since a logarithmic frequency scale is used,
f1 must be greater than 0.

`unwrap` controls phase unwrapping (default True).

For more info, see `plot`.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.nyquist_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response as a Nyquist plot (imaginary part versus
real part).  vvector specifies the  frequencies.  If it
is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits as (f1, f2).

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

The unit circle is shown by default.  This can be disabled
with `unitcircle=False`.

methods.name: lcapy.jfexpr.FrequencyResponseDomainExpression.nichols_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response as a Nichols plot (dB versus phase).
vvector specifies the  frequencies.  If it is a tuple
(f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits as (f1, f2).

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.
Frequency domain expression or symbol.

modules.name: lcapy.ratfun
documentation:
This module provides support for rational functions.

Copyright 2016--2024 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.ratfun.polyroots
documentation: Return roots of polynomial `poly` for variable `var`.
Perhaps have 3 classes to represent responses?
1. RationalFunction            N(s) / D(s)
2. RationalFunctionWithExp     B(s) * exp(-s * T) / D(s)
3. RationalFunctionWithExpSum  Sum(B_m(s) * exp(-s * T_m), m) / D(s)
2 is a generalized form of 1 with T = 0.

functions.name: lcapy.ratfun.roots
documentation: Return roots of expression `expr` for variable `var`.

functions.name: lcapy.ratfun._zp2tf
documentation:
Create a transfer function from lists (or dictionaries) of zeros
and poles, and from a constant gain

functions.name: lcapy.ratfun._tc2tf
documentation:
Create a transfer function in time-constant form from lists (or
dictionaries) of zeros and poles, and from a constant gain

functions.name: lcapy.ratfun._pr2tf
documentation: Create a transfer function from lists of poles and residues.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.__init__
documentation:
This represents a generalized rational function of the form:
`N(var) / D(var) = (B(var) / A(var)) * exp(-var * delay) * U(var)`

where B and D are polynomials in var and U is the product of
undefined functions of var.

Note, delay only represents a delay when var is s.

It does not detect expressions such as:
`B(var) * (1 - exp(-var * delay)) / A(var)

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.roots
documentation:
Return roots of expression as a dictionary
Note this may not find them all.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.zeros
documentation:
Return zeroes of expression as a dictionary
Note this may not find them all.

Zeros at infinity are not returned.  There will be q - p zeros
at infinity for a rational function with a numerator of degree p
and a denominator of degree q.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.poles
documentation:
Return poles of expression as a list of Root objects.
Note this may not find all the poles.

Poles at infinity are not returned.  There will be p - q poles
at infinity for a rational function with a numerator of degree p
and a denominator of degree q.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.residue
documentation: Determine residue for given pole.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.canonical
documentation:
Convert rational function to canonical form; this is like general
form but with a unity highest power of denominator.  For
example,

(5 * s**2 + 5 * s + 5) / (s**2 + 4)

If factor_const is True, factor constants from numerator, for example,

5 * (s**2 + s + 1) / (s**2 + 4)

See also general, partfrac, standard, timeconst, and ZPK

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.general
documentation:
Convert rational function to general form.

See also canonical, partfrac, standard, timeconst, and ZPK

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.expandcanonical
documentation:
Expand in terms for different powers with each term
expressed in canonical form.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.partfrac
documentation:
Convert rational function into partial fraction form.

If combine_conjugates is True then the pair of partial
fractions for complex conjugate poles are combined.

See also canonical, standard, general, timeconst, and ZPK

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.standard
documentation:
Convert rational function into mixed fraction form.

This is the sum of strictly proper rational function and a
polynomial.

See also canonical, general, partfrac, timeconst, and ZPK

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.timeconst
documentation:
Convert rational function to time constant form with unity
lowest power of denominator.

See also canonical, general, partfrac, standard, and ZPK

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.ZPK
documentation:
Convert to zero-pole-gain (ZPK) form.

See also canonical, general, standard, timeconst, and partfrac

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.residues
documentation:
Return residues of partial fraction expansion.

This is not much use without the corresponding poles.
It is better to use as_QRF.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.degree
documentation:
Return the degree (order) of the rational function.

This the maximum of the numerator and denominator degrees.
Note zero has a degree of -inf.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.Ndegree
documentation:
Return the degree (order) of the numerator of a rational function.
Note zero has a degree of -inf.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.Ddegree
documentation:
Return the degree (order) of the denominator of a rational function.
Note zero has a degree of -inf.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.is_strictly_proper
documentation:
Return True if the degree of the dominator is greater
than the degree of the numerator.

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.as_ZPK
documentation:
Decompose expression into zeros, poles, gain, undef where

expression = K * (prod_n (var - z_n) / (prod_n (var - p_n)) * undef

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.as_QMA
documentation:
Decompose expression into Q, M, A, delay, undef where

`expression = (Q + M / A) * exp(-delay * var) * undef`

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.as_QRF_old
documentation:
Decompose expression into Q, R, F, delay, undef where

expression = (Q + sum_n r_n / f_n) * exp(-delay * var) * undef

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.as_QRPO
documentation:
Decompose expression into Q, R, P, O, delay, undef where

`expression = (Q + sum_n r_n / (var - p_n)**o_n) * exp(-delay * var) * undef`

`method` can be 'sub' (substitution method, the default) or
'ec' (equating cofficients method).

methods.name: lcapy.ratfun.Ratfun.as_QRF
documentation:
Decompose expression into Q, R, F, delay, undef where

expression = (Q + sum_n r_n / f_n) * exp(-delay * var) * undef

`method` can be 'sub' (substitution method, the default) or
'ec' (equating cofficients method).

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.inverse_ztransform
documentation:
Attempt inverse Z ransform.

If causal=True the response is zero for n < 0 and
the result is multiplied by UnitStep(n)
If ac=True or dc=True the result is extrapolated for n < 0.
Otherwise the result is only known for n >= 0.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.transient_response
documentation: Evaluate transient (impulse) response.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.impulse_response
documentation: Evaluate transient (impulse) response.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.step_response
documentation: Evaluate step response.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.frequency_response
documentation:
Convert to frequency domain using DTFT and evaluate response if
frequency vector specified.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.response
documentation: Evaluate response to input signal x at times t.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.state_space
documentation:
Create state-space representation from transfer function.  Note,
state-space representations are not unique and are determined
by the `form` argument.  Currently this can be 'CCF' for the
controllable canonical form, 'OCF' for the observable
canonical form, or 'DCF' for the diagonal canonical form.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.ss
documentation:
Return state-space representation using controllable canonical form.
For other forms, use `state_space()`.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot pole-zero map.

kwargs include:
unitcircle - if True, draw unit circle
axes - the plot axes to use otherwise a new figure is created
xlabel - the x-axis label (default Re(z))
ylabel - the y-axis label (default Im(z))
xscale - the x-axis scaling
yscale - the y-axis scaling
in addition to those supported by the matplotlib plot command.

The plot axes are returned.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.bode_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Bode
plot (but without the straight line approximations), assumong
`dt=1`.  fvector specifies the frequencies.  If it is a tuple
(f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits.  Since a logarithmic
frequency scale is used, f1 must be greater than 0.

This method makes the assumption that the expression is causal.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.nyquist_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Nyquist
plot assuming `dt=1`.  fvector specifies the frequencies.  If it is a tuple
(f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits.

The unit circle is shown by default.  This can be disabled with `unitcircle=False`.

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

This method makes the assumption that the expression is causal.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.nichols_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Nichols
plot assuming `dt=1`.  fvector specifies the frequencies.  If
it is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits.

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

This method makes the assumption that the expression is causal.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.inverse_bilinear_transform
documentation:
Approximate z = exp(s * dt)

by z = (1 + 0.5 * dt * s) / (1 - 0.5 * dt * s)

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.DFT
documentation:
Determine DFT.

`N` needs to be a positive integer symbol or a str specifying
the extent of the DFT.  By default `N` is defined as 'N'.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.discrete_time_fourier_transform
documentation: Convert to Fourier domain using discrete time Fourier transform.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.DTFT
documentation: Convert to Fourier domain using discrete time Fourier transform.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.fourier
documentation: Attempt discrete-time Fourier transform. This is an alias for DTFT.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.norm_fourier
documentation: Attempt normalized discrete-time Fourier transform.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.angular_fourier
documentation: Attempt angular discrete-time Fourier transform.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.norm_angular_fourier
documentation: Attempt normalized angular discrete-time Fourier transform.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.as_ab
documentation:
Return lists of denominator and numerator coefficients
when the denominator and numerator are expressed as polynomials
in z**-1.  The lowest order coefficients are returned first.

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.difference_equation
documentation:
Create difference equation from transfer function.

`form` can be 'fir' or 'iir' ('direct form I').

methods.name: lcapy.zexpr.ZDomainExpression.dlti_filter
documentation:
Create discrete-time linear time-invariant filter from
discrete-time transfer function.

If `normalize_a0` is `True` (default) all the coefficients
are normalized so a0 = 1.
z-domain expression or symbol.

Return the equations in matrix form.

Forms can be:
 'default'

If `invert` is True, evaluate the matrix inverse.

documentation:
Determine the unknown voltages using Laplace transforms and
return as a dict

methods.name: lcapy.nodalanalysis.NodalAnalysis.pdb
documentation: Enter the python debugger.

This is for nodal analysis.

```pycon
>>> from lcapy import Circuit, NodalAnalysis
>>> cct = Circuit('''
... V1 1 0 {u(t)}; down
... R1 1 2; right=2
... L1 2 3; down=2
... W1 0 3; right
... W 1 5; up
... W 2 6; up
... C1 5 6; right=2
...''')
```

To perform nodal analysis in the Laplace domain:

```pycon
>>> na = NodalAnalysis(cct.laplace())
```

To display the equations found by applying KCL at each node:

```pycon
>>> na.nodal_equations().pprint()
```

To display the system of equations (in matrix form) that needs to
be solved:

```pycon
>>> na.matrix_equations().pprint()
```

This only works for dc, ac, or laplace domains. For example,

```pycon
>>> NodalAnalysis(cct.laplace()).matrix_equations().pprint()
```

modules.name: lcapy.netlistnamespace
documentation:
This module provides the NetlistNamespace class.

Copyright 2020--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.netlistnamespace.NetlistNamespace

methods.name: lcapy.netlistnamespace.NetlistNamespace.__getitem__
documentation: Return element or node by name.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistnamespace.NetlistNamespace.__getattr__
documentation:
Return element, node, or another NetlistNamespace object by name.
This gets called if there is no explicit attribute attr for
this instance.  This is primarily for accessing elements and
non-numerical node names.  It also gets called if the called
attr throws an AttributeError exception.  The annoying thing
is that hasattr uses getattr and checks for an exception.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistnamespace.NetlistNamespace.components
documentation: Return ordered dictionary of components.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistnamespace.NetlistNamespace.netlist
documentation: Return the current netlist for this namespace.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistnamespace.NetlistNamespace.sch
documentation: Generate schematic for this namespace.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistnamespace.NetlistNamespace.draw
documentation:
Draw schematic of subnetlist.

filename specifies the name of the file to produce.  If None,
the schematic is displayed on the screen.

Note, if using Jupyter, then need to first issue command %matplotlib inline

kwargs include:
   label_ids: True to show component ids
   label_values: True to display component values
   draw_nodes:
      True to show all nodes,
      False or 'none' to show no nodes,
      'primary' to show primary nodes,
      'connections' to show nodes that connect more than two components,
      'all' to show all nodes
   label_nodes:
       True to label all nodes,
       False or 'none' to label no nodes,
       'primary' to label primary nodes,
       'alpha' to label nodes starting with a letter,
       'pins' to label nodes that are pins on a chip,
       'all' to label all nodes
   style: 'american', 'british', or 'european'
   scale: schematic scale factor, default 1.0
   node_spacing: spacing between component nodes, default 2.0
   cpt_size: size of a component, default 1.5
   dpi: dots per inch for png files
   help_lines: distance between lines in grid, default 0.0 (disabled)
   debug: True to display debug information

This class allows elements, nodes, or other namespaces
in a heirachical namespace to be accessed by name, via \_\_getattr\_\_.

For example,

```pycon
>>> a = Circuit('''
... b.L1 1 2'''
>>> a.b.L1.v
```

methods.name: lcapy.netlistmixin.NetlistMixin.independent_source_groups
documentation:
Return list of source groups.  Each group is a list of
sourcenames that can be analysed at the same time.  Noise
sources have separate groups since they are assumed to be
uncorrelated.  The source groups are keyed by the names of
superposition decomposition keys: 'dc', 's', 't', 'n*', and omega,
where omega is an expression for the angular frequency of a phasor,
and `n*` is a noise identifier.

If transform is False, the returned keys are 'dc', 't', 's', and 'n*'.

If transform is True, the returned keys are 'dc', 's', omega,
and 'n*'.  Note, there is no time-domain component.  Note,
after transformation, a source can appear in multiple groups.
For example, if a voltage source V1 has a value 10 + 5 *
cos(omega * t), V1 will be added to the dc and omega groups.

methods.name: lcapy.netlistmixin.NetlistMixin.draw
documentation:
Draw schematic of netlist.

filename specifies the name of the file to produce.  If None,
the schematic is displayed on the screen.

Note, if using Jupyter, then need to first issue command %matplotlib inline

kwargs include:
   label_ids: True to show component ids
   label_values: True to display component values
   draw_nodes: True to show all nodes, False to show no nodes,
     'primary' to show primary nodes,
     'connections' to show nodes that connect more than two components,
     'all' to show all nodes
   label_nodes: True to label all nodes, False to label no nodes,
     'primary' to label primary nodes,
     'alpha' to label nodes starting with a letter,
     'pins' to label nodes that are pins on a chip,
     'all' to label all nodes
   style: 'american', 'british', or 'european'
   scale: schematic scale factor, default 1.0
   node_spacing: spacing between component nodes, default 2.0
   cpt_size: size of a component, default 1.5
   dpi: dots per inch for png files
   help_lines: distance between lines in grid, default 0.0 (disabled)
   debug: True to display debug information

classes.name: lcapy.schemlineqplacer.Constraint
documentation: This is an experimental component placement algorithm for schematics
based on solving a system of linear equations.  The unknowns are the
node positions and the stretch requried for stretchy components.
The algorithm identifies the free stretch variables and assigns them
to be zero.  However, this depends on the constraint ordering and
so sometimes a negative stretch is generated due to a poor choice
of basic variable.  This could be avoided with a constrained
optimisation where the node positions and component stretches all
need to be non-negative.

methods.name: lcapy.mna.MNA.matrix_equations
documentation:
System of equations used to find the unknowns.

Forms can be:
 A y = b
 b = A y
 Ainv b = y
 y = Ainv b

If `invert` is True, evaluate the matrix inverse.

methods.name: lcapy.mna.MNA.equations
documentation:
System of equations used to find the unknowns.

If inverse is True, evaluate the matrix inverse.

This is for compatibility and is deprecated.  Use
matrix_equations instead.

This class performs modified nodal analysis (MNA) on a netlist of
components.  There are several variants:

1. DC analysis if all the independent sources are DC.  The .V and .I
methods return DC expressions with the dc assumption set.

2. AC analysis if all the independent sources are AC.  The .V and .I
methods return phasors.

3. Initial value Laplace analysis if an L or C has an explicit
initial value.  The .V and .I methods return s-domain expressions
with no assumption sets; thus the time-domain results are only
valid for t >= 0.

4. General Laplace analysis.  If all the sources are causal and
all the initial conditions are zero (explicitly or implicitly)
then the time-domain results are causal.

5. Noise analysis.

Note, it is assumed that the user of this class uses superposition
to solve problems with mixed independent sources, such as DC and
AC.

This module provides support for Hilbert transforms.  It calculates

classes.name: lcapy.hilbert.HilbertTransformer
documentation: If g(t) has a Hilbert transform H g(t) then
g(t - t0) has a Hilbert transform H g(t - t0) and
g(a t)has a Hilbert transform sign(a) H g(a t).
H(g(t) * h(t)) = H g(t) * h(t) = g(t) * H h(t).
H dg(t)/dt = d H g(t) dt

modules.name: lcapy.fourier
documentation:
This module provides support for Fourier transforms.  It calculates
the bilateral Fourier transform using:

   S(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s(t) e^{-j * 2 * \pi * t} dt

It also allows functions that strictly do not have a Fourier transform
by using Dirac deltas.  For example, a, cos(a * t), sin(a * t), exp(j
* a * t).


Copyright 2016--2023 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.fourier.FT
documentation:
Compute bilateral Fourier transform of expr.

Undefined functions such as v(t) are converted to V(f)

This also handles some expressions that do not really have a Fourier
transform, such as a, cos(a * t), sin(a * t), exp(I * a * t).

functions.name: lcapy.fourier.fourier_transform
documentation:
Compute bilateral Fourier transform of expr.

Undefined functions such as v(t) are converted to V(f)

This also handles some expressions that do not really have a Fourier
transform, such as a, cos(a * t), sin(a * t), exp(I * a * t).

modules.name: lcapy.kseq
documentation:
This module provides the DiscreteFourierDomainSequence class to
represent discrete-Fourier domain sequences.

Copyright 2021 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.kseq.kseq
documentation:
Create a discrete-Fourier domain Sequence from a tuple, list, ndarray, or str.

```pycon
>>> a = kseq((1, 2, 3))
```

The sequence indices are specified with the optional `ni` argument.
For example:

```pycon
>>> a = kseq((1, 2, 3, 4), (-1, 0, 1, 2))
```

If the `ni` argument is not specified, the sequence indices
are enumerated from 0.

With a string argument, an underscore indicates the zero sequence
index:

```pycon
>>> a = kseq('{1, _2, 3, 4}')
```

The sequence indices can be found using the `n` attribute.
This returns a list.

```pycon
>>> a = kseq('{1, _2, 3, 4}').n
[-1, 0, 1, 2]
```

modules.name: lcapy.transform
documentation:
This module performs transformations between domains.

Copyright 2018--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.transform.transform
documentation:
If arg is a domain variable perform domain transformation,
otherwise perform substitution.

Note (1 / s)(omega) will fail since 1 / s is assumed not to be
causal and so the Fourier transform is unknown.  However,
impedance(1 / s)(omega) will work since an impedance is assumed to
be causal.  Alternatively, use (1 / s)(omega, causal=True).

Transforming from s->jomega is fast since it just requires
a substitution of s with jomega.

Transforming from s->omega, s->Omega, s->f, or s->F can be slow since
this requires a inverse Laplace transform followed by a Fourier
transform.  However, if the expression is causal and the
expression is lossy when s is replaced by jw, the result can be
found by substituting jw or 2 * 2 * pi * f for s.  This does not
apply for an expression such as Z = 1 / (s * C).

methods.name: lcapy.ltifilter.LTIFilter.response
documentation:
Calculate response of filter to input `x` given a list of initial conditions
`ic` for time indexes specified by `ni`.  If `ni` is a tuple,
this specifies the first and last (inclusive) time index.

The initial conditions are valid prior to the time indices given by the ni
`x` can be an expression, a sequence, or a list/array of values.

methods.name: lcapy.ltifilter.LTIFilter.pdb
documentation: Enter the python debugger.

methods.name: lcapy.ltifilter.LTIFilter.discretize
documentation:
Convert to a discrete-time linear time-invariant filter.

The default method is 'bilinear'.  Other methods are:
'impulse-invariance' 'bilinear', 'tustin', 'trapezoidal'
'generalized-bilinear', 'gbf' controlled by the parameter
`alpha`, 'euler', 'forward-diff', 'forward-euler'
'backward-diff', 'backward-euler' 'simpson', 'matched-Z',
'zero-pole-matching'

methods.name: lcapy.ltifilter.LTIFilter.is_stable
documentation:
Return True if all the poles of the filters transfer function
are within the unit circle.

See also is_marginally_stable.

methods.name: lcapy.ltifilter.LTIFilter.is_marginally_stable
documentation:
Return True if all the poles of the filters transfer function

methods.name: lcapy.kexpr.DiscreteFourierDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response at values specified by kvector.  If kvector
is a tuple, this sets the frequency limits.

kwargs include:
axes - the plot axes to use otherwise a new figure is created
xlabel - the x-axis label
ylabel - the y-axis label
ylabel2 - the second y-axis label if needed, say for mag and phase
xscale - the x-axis scaling, say for plotting as ms
yscale - the y-axis scaling, say for plotting mV
plot_type -  'dB_phase', 'mag-phase', 'real-imag', 'mag', 'phase',
'real', or 'imag'
in addition to those supported by the matplotlib plot command.

The plot axes are returned.

There are many plotting options, see lcapy.plot and
matplotlib.pyplot.plot.

For example:
    V.plot(kvector, log_frequency=True)
    V.real.plot(kvector, color='black')
    V.phase.plot(kvector, color='black', linestyle='--')

By default complex data is plotted as separate plots of magnitude (dB)
and phase.

documentation:
Determine inverse DFT.

`N` needs to be a positive integer symbol or a str specifying
the extent of the inverse DFT.  By default `N` is defined as
'N'.
Discrete Fourier domain expression or symbol.

modules.name: lcapy.conductance
documentation:
This module provides conductance support.

Copyright 2021--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.conductance.conductance
documentation:
Create an admittance class for the specified conductance.

The conductance, G, is defined as the real part of the admittance:

Y(omega) = G(omega) + j B(omega)

modules.name: lcapy.seq
documentation:
This module creates sequences.

Copyright 2020--2024 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.seq.seq
documentation:
Create a Sequence from a tuple, list, ndarray, or str.

```pycon
>>> a = seq((1, 2, 3))
```

The sequence indices are specified with the optional `ni` argument.
For example:

```pycon
>>> a = seq((1, 2, 3, 4), (-1, 0, 1, 2))
```

If the `ni` argument is not specified, the sequence indices are
enumerated from -`origin` with `origin` having a default value of
0\. `origin` defines the element for `n = 0`. For example,

```pycon
>>> seq((1, 2, 3), origin=1)
{1, _2, 3}
```

With a string argument, an underscore indicates the zero sequence
index:

```pycon
>>> a = seq('{1, _2, 3, 4}')
```

The sequence indices can be found using the `n` attribute.
This returns a list.

```pycon
>>> seq('{1, _2, 3, 4}').n
[-1, 0, 1, 2]
```

This function creates a discrete-time domain sequence. See also
`nseq` to create a discrete-time domain sequence, `kseq` to create
a discrete-Fourier domain sequence, and `zseq` to create a
z-domain sequence.

modules.name: lcapy.noisefexpr
documentation:
This module provides the FourierNoiseDomainExpression class to represent
f-domain (Fourier domain) noise expressions.

Copyright 2014--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.noisefexpr.FourierNoiseDomainExpression

methods.name: lcapy.noisefexpr.FourierNoiseDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response at values specified by fvector.

There are many plotting options, see matplotlib.pyplot.plot.

For example:
    V.plot(fvector, log_frequency=True)
    V.real.plot(fvector, color='black')
    V.phase.plot(fvector, color='black', linestyle='--')

By default complex data is plotted as separate plots of magnitude (dB)
and phase.

methods.name: lcapy.noisefexpr.FourierNoiseDomainExpression.transform
documentation: Transform into a different domain.

Frequency domain (one-sided) noise spectrum expression (amplitude
spectral density).

This characterises a wide-sense stationary, zero-mean Gaussian
noise random process.

When performing arithmetic on two FourierNoiseDomainExpression
expressions it is assumed that they are uncorrelated unless they
have the same nid (noise indentifier).  If the nid is not
specified, a new one is created.

Uncorrelated noise expressions are added in quadrature (on a power
basis).  Thus (FourierNoiseDomainExpression(3) +
FourierNoiseDomainExpression(4)).expr = 5 since 5 = sqrt(3**2 +
4**2)

FourierNoiseDomainExpression(3) != FourierNoiseDomainExpression(3)
since they are different noise realisations albeit with the same
properties.  However, FourierNoiseDomainExpression(3).expr ==
FourierNoiseDomainExpression(3).expr.  Similarly,
FourierNoiseDomainExpression(3, nid='n1') ==
FourierNoiseDomainExpression(3, nid='n1') since they have the same
noise identifier and thus have the same realisation.

Caution: The sum of two noise expressions generates a noise
expression with a new nid.  This can lead to unexpected results
since noise expressions with different nids are assumed to be
uncorrelated.  For example, consider:
a = FourierNoiseDomainExpression(3);
b = FourierNoiseDomainExpression(4)
a + b - b gives sqrt(41) but a + b - a gives sqrt(34).

This case is correctly handled by the SuperpositionVoltage and
SuperpositionCurrent classes since each noise component is stored
and considered separately.

(SuperpositionVoltage(a) + SuperpositionVoltage(b) -
SuperpositionVoltage(b)).n gives 3 as expected.

classes.name: lcapy.noisefexpr.FourierNoiseDomainVoltage
documentation:
Voltage noise amplitude spectral density (units V/rtHz).
This can be a function of linear frequency, f.  For example,
to model an opamp voltage noise:

v = FourierNoiseDomainVoltage(1e-8 / sqrt(f) + 8e-9)

classes.name: lcapy.noisefexpr.FourierNoiseDomainCurrent
documentation:
Current noise amplitude spectral density (units A/rtHz).

This can be a function of linear frequency, f.  For example,
to model an opamp current noise:

i = FourierNoiseDomainCurrent(3e-12 / sqrt(f) + 200e-15)

modules.name: lcapy.assumptions
documentation:
This module provides the Assumptions class.  It manages
assumptions for expressions needed for inverse Laplace transforms
and expression simplification.

Copyright 2014--2020 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.assumptions.Assumptions

methods.name: lcapy.assumptions.Assumptions.sympy_assumptions
documentation: Return dict of the SymPy assumptions such as complex, positive, etc.

methods.name: lcapy.assumptions.Assumptions.merge_and_infer
documentation:
Override assumptions with specified assumptions.
If none specified, infer them.

Manage expression assumptions.  These include 'ac', 'dc', 'causal'
and 'unknown' to specify expression behavior for t < 0, 'positive',
'complex', etc., to help with expression simplification, 'nid' as a noise
identifier, and `omega` for the phasor angular frequency.

'ac', 'dc', 'causal' and 'unknown' refer to the time domain
expression.  They can be inferred for a time domain expression but
can be overridden.  The inference is performed lazily.  In
particular, it is performed when converting from the time domain
to the Laplace or Phasor domains.  The latter is required to
handle the case of conversions from the Phasor to the Laplace
domain.  Conversions from the Fourier domain to the Laplace domain
go via the time domain and so the inference is performed while in
the time domain.

modules.name: lcapy.dft
documentation:
This module provides support for discrete Fourier transforms.

It calculates the discrete Fourier transform using:

   X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j * 2 * \pi * n * k / N}

Copyright 2020--2021 Michael Hayes, UCECE
Juergen Weizenecker, HKA

functions.name: lcapy.dft.is_in_interval
documentation:
Check if bin belongs to an interval [n0, n1].
 n0, n1, nk may contain parameter

This returns:
 -1  if bin is not in interval and is on the left side
  0  if bin is in interval
  1  if bin is not in interval and is on the right side

If n0, n1 or nk contain parameter, 0 is returned if no decision is
possible and a comment is printed.

functions.name: lcapy.dft.find_pow
documentation:
This finds terms of the form base**NN in the expression q_expr.
It is used for simplifying q**N terms to one if q=exp(-+j*2*pi/N*k).

It returns a list of all bases found e.g., [q/3, q/5, 2q, aq,  .......].

functions.name: lcapy.dft.special_diff
documentation:
Make derivative of a**(k-s)*Q / (1-a**N)**o with respect to a, and
divide result by a**(k-s-1)/(1-a**N)**(o+1) to obtain a
polynom in n.  This is used with make_polynom_table.

functions.name: lcapy.dft.make_polynom_table1
documentation:
This Table contains solutions of the DFT of
exp(j*2*pi/N*n*mu) / (1-a*exp(j*2*pi/N*n))**p
where mu < p  and mu, p are integer
NO roots in the denominator

use the table as follows
the list Qpoly[p] contains polynoms with respect to k of the solutions to a specific p and  0<=mu<=p-1 .
The full solution is obtained as
N * a**(k-mu) / (1-a**N)**p / (p-1) *Qpoly[p][mu]           p >= 2
N*a**k / (1-a**N)* TP[1]                                                       p =1

 Example for p=3
 1 / (1-a*exp(-j*2*pi/N*n))**3  = N*a**k * Qpoly[3][0] / (1-a**N)**3 / 2
 exp(-j*2*pi/N*n) / (1-a*exp(-j*2*pi/N*n))**3  = N*a**(k-1) * Qpoly[3][1] / (1-a**N)**3 / 2
 exp(-j*2*pi/N*n*2) / (1-a*exp(-j*2*pi/N*n))**3  = N*a**(k-2) * Qpoly[3][2] / (1-a**N)**3 / 2

The results for all p's are calculated (--> Qpoly), only p=MaxP is returned.
The parameter a and N can be set, if not a and N remain a symbol

functions.name: lcapy.dft.make_polynom_table2
documentation:
This Table contains solutions of the DFT of
exp(j*2*pi/N*n*mu) / (1-a*exp(j*2*pi/N*n))**p
where mu < p  and mu, p are integer
a is of the form exp(-j*2*pi*r/N), so here we have roots in the denominator at n=r

The solution is of the form
    Q[mu] * exp(-j*2*pi*r/N*(k-mu)), were Q[mu] is a polynom in k of order p

    idea q = exp(j*2*pi/N*(n-r)):
   The transform of C_p(q) / (1-q)**p is (k**p - 1/N*sum_0_N-1(k**p)) * exp(-j*2*pi*mu*k/N)                 (1)

   Find from these the transforms of 1/(1-q)**p, q/(1-q)**p, ...., q**(p-1)/(1-q)**p by an appropriate
   linear combination of the transforms in (1).
   The C_p(q) is the polynom according to  sum_0_(N-1) n**p * q**n = N*Cp / (1-q)**p with q**N=1

functions.name: lcapy.dft.simp_rat
documentation:
Make a few simplification steps for a rational function, useful for DFT's
method 1: uses cancel
method 2: uses factor_list of num and denom and cancel their common factors by hand
Does not use simplify as sometimes weird expression may be generated
e.g. exp(j*2*pi/N) = (-1)**(2/N), or denoms are expanded and not
factorized or it takes to long

functions.name: lcapy.dft.discrete_fourier_transform
documentation:
Compute bilateral discrete Fourier transform of expr.

Undefined functions such as x(n) are converted to X(k)

functions.name: lcapy.dft.DFT
documentation:
Compute bilateral discrete Fourier transform of expr.

Undefined functions such as x(n) are converted to X(k)

documentation: Return DFT matrix of size `N` x `N`.

documentation: Delete special cases.

methods.name: lcapy.dft.QkTransform.shift_k

methods.name: lcapy.dft.QkTransform.add
documentation: Merge (add) two QkTransforms.

methods.name: lcapy.dft.QkTransform.subs
documentation: Substitute q by a new expression.

Simplifies ba**NN to 1

If all is False, only the special cases will regarded.
Replace e.g., (q/3)**NN by (1/3)**N

methods.name: lcapy.dft.QkTransform.make_transform
documentation:
Assemble the final tranform from all the cases, the general case, or Xk.

backward is True for IDFT
q is replaced by sub

This handles X(q) transforms, where

X(q) = sum_lower^upper x(n) * q**N

As special cases are possible (e.g., for q=0) the class handles the
general expression and special cases

methods.name: lcapy.dft.DFTTransformer.termXq
documentation: Make transform  Xq = sum_lower^upper  x[n] * q**n

methods.name: lcapy.dft.DFTTransformer.termXk
documentation:
Make transform  X = sum_0^N-1  x[n] * exp(-2*pi*j*k*n/N)
BUT result can not be written as function of exp(-2*pi*j*k/N)
This part will be mainly important and used with the inverse DFT

In general the terms implemented have the form:

   exp(j*2*pi/N*n*mu)/(1-a*exp(j*2*pi/N*n))**p      with mu integer

   or exp(-j*2*pi/N*k*mu)/(1-a*exp(-j*2*pi/N*k))**p

modules.name: lcapy.schemgraphplacer
documentation:
This module performs schematic component placement using a graph algorithm.

Copyright 2021--2023 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.schemgraphplacer.SchemGraphPlacer
documentation: Components are positioned using two graphs; one graph for
the x direction and the other for the y direction.
There is a naming confusion.  We have network nodes (electrical
nodes) and nodes in the graph used for component placement.
Let's call the latter gnodes.  The names of these gnodes are a
tuple of the common electrical nodes.
x and y component positioning are performed independently.  Let's
consider the x or horizontal positioning.  There are three stages:
1. Component nodes that share a y position are linked; this can
occur, for example, for a vertically oriented component.
This helps to reduce the size of the graph.
2. The x positions of the components are used to determine the
graph edges.
3. The longest path through the graph is found and the x positions
of the nodes are assigned based on the distance along the
longest path.

modules.name: lcapy.immittancemixin
documentation:
This module provides the ImmittanceMixin class.  It provides common
methods for immittances.

Copyright 2021 Michael Hayes, UCECE

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.R
documentation: Resistance.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.resistance
documentation: Resistance.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.is_lossy
documentation: Has some none zero resistance component.

documentation: Reactance.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.reactance
documentation: Reactance.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.G
documentation:
Conductance.

Note Y = G + j * B = 1 / Z = 1 / (R + j * X)
and so G = R / (R**2 + X**2).

Thus for DC, when X = 0, then G = 1 / R and is infinite for R
= 0.  However, if Z is purely imaginary, i.e, R = 0 then G = 0,
not infinity as might be expected.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.conductance
documentation: Conductance.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.B
documentation: Susceptance.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.Y
documentation:
Admittance.

The admittance is expressed in jomega form for AC circuits
and in s-domain for for transient circuits.

Use Y(omega) or Y(s) to achieve the desired form.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.Z
documentation:
Impedance.

The impedance is expressed in jomega form for AC circuits
and in s-domain for for transient circuits.

Use Z(omega) or Z(s) to achieve the desired form.

methods.name: lcapy.immittancemixin.ImmittanceMixin.network
documentation:
Synthesise a network with an equivalent impedance.  `form`
includes: cauerI, cauerII, fosterI, fosterII, seriesRLC,
seriesGC, parallelRLC, seriesRC, seriesRL, parallelRC,
parallelRL, parallelGC.  The default is CauerI.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.inverse_laplace
documentation:
Attempt inverse Laplace transform.

If causal=True the response is zero for t < 0 and
the result is multiplied by Heaviside(t)
If ac=True or dc=True the result is extrapolated for t < 0.
Otherwise the result is only known for t >= 0.

By default initial conditions are assumed to be zero.  This
can be controlled by `zero_initial_conditions`.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.ILT
documentation:
Attempt inverse Laplace transform.

If causal=True the response is zero for t < 0 and
the result is multiplied by Heaviside(t)
If ac=True or dc=True the result is extrapolated for t < 0.
Otherwise the result is only known for t >= 0.

By default initial conditions are assumed to be zero.  This
can be controlled by `zero_initial_conditions`.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.time
documentation:
Convert to time domain.

If causal=True the response is zero for t < 0 and
the result is multiplied by Heaviside(t)
If ac=True or dc=True the result is extrapolated for t < 0.
Otherwise the result is only known for t >= 0.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.laplace
documentation: Convert to s-domain.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.fourier
documentation: Convert to Fourier domain.

documentation:
Convert to frequency response domain.  Note, this is similar to the
Fourier domain but not always.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.angular_frequency_response
documentation:
Convert to angular frequency response domain.  Note, this is

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression._response_impulse_invariance
documentation:
Evaluate response of system with applied signal using impulse
invariance method.  This method assumes that the data is well
over-sampled and that there are no Dirac deltas.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression._response_bilinear
documentation:
Evaluate response of system with applied signal using bilinear
method.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.response
documentation:
Evaluate response to input signal `xvector` at times
specified by `tvector`.  This returns a NumPy array.

The default method is 'bilinear'.  Other methods are:
'impulse-invariance'
'bilinear', 'tustin', 'trapezoidal'
'generalized-bilinear', 'gbf' controlled by the parameter `alpha`
'euler', 'forward-diff', 'forward-euler'
'backward-diff', 'backward-euler'.

See also `discretize` with regards to scaling of the result.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.state_space
documentation:
Create state-space representation from transfer function.  Note,
state-space representations are not unique and are determined
by the `form` argument.  Currently this can be 'CCF' for the
controllable canonical form, 'OCF' for the observable
canonical form, or 'DCF' for the diagonal canonical form.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.ss
documentation:
Return state-space representation using controllable canonical form.
For other forms, use `state_space()`.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.differential_equation
documentation:
Create differential equation from transfer function.

For example,
\>>> H = (s + 3) / (s\*\*2 + 4)
\>>> H.differential_equation()

> d d

y(t) + --(y(t)) = 4.x(t) + ---(x(t))
: dt 2

  : dt

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.lti_filter
documentation:
Create continuous-time linear time-invariant filter from
continuous-time transfer function.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.dlti_filter
documentation: Create DLTI filter using bilinear transform.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot pole-zero map.

kwargs include:
axes - the plot axes to use otherwise a new figure is created
xlabel - the x-axis label (default Re(s))
ylabel - the y-axis label (default Im(s))
xscale - the x-axis scaling
yscale - the y-axis scaling
pole_color -- the color of the pole markers
zero_color -- the color of the zero markers
in addition to those supported by the matplotlib plot command.

The plot axes are returned.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.pole_zero_plot
documentation: Plot pole-zero map.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.bode_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Bode
plot (but without the straight line approximations).

If `var` is None or `f`, `fvector` specifies the (linear)
frequencies (in hertz) otherwise if `var` is `omega`,
`fvector` specifies the angular frequencies (in radians).

 If `fvector` is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency
limits.  Since a logarithmic frequency scale is used, f1 must
be greater than 0.

`unwrap` controls phase unwrapping (default True).

This method makes the assumption that the expression is causal.
Note, the Bode plot does not exist for marginally stable and unstable
systems since `jw` is outside the region of convergence.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.nyquist_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Nyquist
plot (imaginary part versus real part).  fvector specifies the
frequencies.  If it is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency
limits.

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

The unit circle is shown by default.  This can be disabled with `unitcircle=False`.

This method makes the assumption that the expression is causal.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.nichols_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Nichols
plot (dB versus phase).  fvector specifies the frequencies.
If it is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits.

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

This method makes the assumption that the expression is causal.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.generalized_bilinear_transform
documentation:
Approximate s = ln(z) / dt

by s = (1 / dt) * (1 - z**-1) / (alpha + (1 - alpha) * z**-1))

When `alpha = 0` this is equivalent to the forward Euler
(forward difference) method.

When `alpha = 0.5` this is equivalent to the bilinear
transform, aka, Tustin's method or the trapezoidal method.

When `alpha = 1` this is equivalent to the backward Euler
(backward difference) method.

See also `discretize` with regards to scaling of the result.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.bilinear_transform
documentation:
Approximate s = ln(z) / dt

by s = (2 / dt) * (1 - z**-1) / (1 + z**-1)

This is also called Tustin's method and is equivalent to the
trapezoidal method.

See also `discretize` with regards to scaling of the result.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.forward_euler_transform
documentation:
Approximate s = ln(z) / dt

by s = (1 / dt) * (1 - z**-1) / z**-1.   This is also known
as the forward difference method.

See also `discretize` with regards to scaling of the result.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.backward_euler_transform
documentation:
Approximate s = ln(z) / dt

by s = (1 / dt) * (1 - z**-1).  This is also known
as the backward difference method.

See also `discretize` with regards to scaling of the result.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.simpson_transform
documentation:
Approximate s = ln(z) / dt

by s = (3 / dt) * (z**2 - 1) / (z**2 + 4 * z + 1).  This is
more accurate than the other methods but doubles the system
order and can produce unstable poles.

See also `discretize` with regards to scaling of the result.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.matched_ztransform
documentation:
Match poles and zeros of H(s) to approximate H(z).

If there are no zeros, this is equivalent to impulse_invariance.

See also `bilinear_transform` and `impulse_invariance_transform`.

See also `discretize` with regards to scaling of the result.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.impulse_invariance_transform
documentation:
This samples the impulse response and then calculates the
Z-transform.  It does not work if the impulse response
has Dirac deltas, say for a transfer function that is
a pure delay or is not-strictly proper.

The discrete-time and continuous-time impulse responses
are identical at the sampling instants n * dt.

The data needs to be sampled many times the bandwidth to avoid
aliasing.

See also `bilinear_transform` and `matched_ztransform`.

See also `discretize` with regards to scaling of the result.

methods.name: lcapy.sexpr.LaplaceDomainExpression.discretize
documentation:
Convert to a discrete-time approximation in the z-domain:

$H(z) pprox K H_c(s)$

where $K$ is a scale factor.

Note, the default scaling is different for admittance,
impedance, transfer function, or undefined expressions
compared to voltage and current expressions.

The scaling is chosen so that the discrete-time voltage and
current expressions have plots similar to the continuous-time
forms. For example, with the impulse-invariance method for a
continuous-time voltage signal $v_c(t)$:

$v[n] = v_c(n \Delta t)$

However, for a transfer-function with a continuous-time
impulse response $h_c(t)$, then

$h[n] = \Delta t h_c(n \Delta t)$.

This corrects the scaling when approximating a continuous-time
convolution by a discrete-time convolution.

The default method is 'bilinear'. Other methods are:
'impulse-invariance' 'bilinear', 'tustin', 'trapezoidal'
'generalized-bilinear', 'gbf' controlled by the parameter
`alpha`, 'euler', 'forward-diff', 'forward-euler'
'backward-diff', 'backward-euler' 'simpson', 'matched-Z',
'zero-pole-matching'
s-domain expression or symbol.

modules.name: lcapy.dtft
documentation:
This module provides support for discrete-time Fourier transforms.

 It calculates the discrete-time Fourier transform using:

   X_{1 / T}(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) e^{-j * 2 * \pi * n * T * f}

where T = \delta t.

Note, there are other definitions, for example:

   X_{1 / T}(f) = T \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) e^{-j * 2 * \pi * n * T * f}

The first version is consistent with the DFT from a units point of view.

Copyright 2021--2024 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.dtft.discrete_time_fourier_transform
documentation:
Compute bilateral discrete-time Fourier transform of expr.

Undefined functions such as x(n) are converted to X(f)

functions.name: lcapy.dtft.DTFT
documentation:
Compute bilateral discrete-time Fourier transform of expr.

Undefined functions such as x(n) are converted to X(f)

modules.name: lcapy.noiseomegaexpr
documentation:
This module provides the AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression class to represent omega-domain (angular Fourier domain) noise expressions.

Copyright 2014--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.noiseomegaexpr.AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression

methods.name: lcapy.noiseomegaexpr.AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response at values specified by omegavector.

There are many plotting options, see matplotlib.pyplot.plot.

For example:
    V.plot(omegavector, log_frequency=True)
    V.real.plot(omegavector, color='black')
    V.phase.plot(omegavector, color='black', linestyle='--')

By default complex data is plotted as separate plots of magnitude (dB)
and phase.

methods.name: lcapy.noiseomegaexpr.AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression.transform
documentation: Transform into a different domain.

Angular frequency domain (one-sided) noise spectrum expression (amplitude
spectral density).

This characterises a wide-sense stationary, zero-mean Gaussian
noise random process.

When performing arithmetic on two AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression
expressions it is assumed that they are uncorrelated unless they have
the same nid (noise indentifier).  If the nid is not specified, a new one is created.

Uncorrelated noise expressions are added in quadrature (on a power
basis).  Thus (AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(3) +
AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(4)).expr = 5 since 5 =
sqrt(3**2 + 4**2)

AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(3) !=
AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(3) since they are different
noise realisations albeit with the same properties.  However,
AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(3).expr ==
AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(3).expr.  Similarly,
AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(3, nid='n1') ==
AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(3, nid='n1') since they have
the same noise identifier and thus have the same realisation.

Caution: The sum of two noise expressions generates a noise
expression with a new nid.  This can lead to unexpected results
since noise expressions with different nids are assumed to be
uncorrelated.  For example, consider:
a = AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(3);
b = AngularFourierNoiseDomainExpression(4)
a + b - b gives sqrt(41) and a + b - a gives sqrt(34).

This case is correctly handled by the SuperpositionVoltage and
SuperpositionCurrent classes since each noise component is stored
and considered separately.

(SuperpositionVoltage(a) + SuperpositionVoltage(b) - SuperpositionVoltage(b)).n gives 3 as expected.

classes.name: lcapy.noiseomegaexpr.AngularFourierNoiseDomainVoltage
documentation:
Voltage noise amplitude spectral density (units V/rtrad/s).
This can be a function of angular frequency, omega.  For example,
to model an opamp voltage noise:

v = AngularFourierNoiseDomainVoltage(1e-8 / sqrt(omega) + 8e-9)

classes.name: lcapy.noiseomegaexpr.AngularFourierNoiseDomainCurrent
documentation:
Current noise amplitude spectral density (units A/rtrad/s).

This can be a function of angular frequency, omega.  For example,
to model an opamp current noise:

i = AngularFourierNoiseDomainCurrent(3e-12 / sqrt(omega) + 200e-15)

modules.name: lcapy.inverse_fourier
documentation:
This module provides support for inverse Fourier transforms.  It
calculates the bilateral inverse Fourier transform using:

   s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(f) e^{j * 2 * \pi * t} df

It also allows functions that strictly do not have an inverse Fourier
transform by using Dirac deltas.

Copyright 2016--2021 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.inverse_fourier.IFT
documentation:
Compute bilateral inverse Fourier transform of expr.

Undefined functions such as V(f) are converted to v(t)

This also handles some expressions that do not really have an
inverse Fourier transform, such as a, cos(a * f), sin(a * f), exp(I *
a * f).

functions.name: lcapy.inverse_fourier.inverse_fourier_transform
documentation:
Compute bilateral inverse Fourier transform of expr.

Undefined functions such as V(f) are converted to v(t)

This also handles some expressions that do not really have an
inverse Fourier transform, such as a, cos(a * f), sin(a * f), exp(I *
a * f).

functions.name: lcapy.approximate.approximate_exp
documentation:
Approximate exp(a) with Pade approximant.  The best time-domain
response (without a jump) is achieved with 'ndegree == ddegree -
1'.  The best frequency-domain response is achieved with ndegree
== ddegree.

functions.name: lcapy.approximate.approximate_hyperbolic_trig
documentation: Approximate cosh(a), sinh(a), tanh(a).

functions.name: lcapy.approximate.approximate_dominant
documentation:
Approximate expression using ball-park numerical values for the
symbols to decide which terms in a sum dominate the sum.  A term
is neglected if its absolute value is below `threshold` times the
maximum absolute value of the terms in the sum.

`defs` is a dict, set, list, or tuple of symbol definitions.

documentation:
Approximate expression by reducing degree of polynomial to
specified degree.

functions.name: lcapy.approximate.approximate_taylor

functions.name: lcapy.sequence.parse_seq_str
documentation: Perhaps subclass numpy ndarray?  But then could not have symbolic

methods.name: lcapy.sequence.Sequence.__init__
documentation:
Sequences can be created from an tuple, list, or ndarray.

See `seq()` to create a Sequence from a string.

```pycon
>>> a = Sequence((1, 2, 3))
```

The sequence indices are specified with the optional `ni` argument.
For example:

```pycon
>>> a = Sequence((1, 2, 3, 4), (-1, 0, 1, 2))
```

If the `ni` argument is not specified, the sequence indices
are enumerated from 0.

The sequence indices can be found using the `n` attribute.
This returns a list.

```pycon
>>> a = Sequence('{1, _2, 3, 4}').n
[-1, 0, 1, 2]
```

Sequences can be converted into discrete-time, discrete-frequency,
z-domain sequences using call notation, for example:

```
>>> a(z)
```

`start_trunc` indicates that the start of the sequence was truncated
`end_trunc` indicates that the end of the sequence was truncated

`start_trunc` and `end_trunc` are not propagated if a sequence is
modified. They indicate that ellipsis should be printed to show
the sequence has been truncated.

methods.name: lcapy.sequence.Sequence.discrete_time_fourier_transform
documentation: Convert to Fourier domain using discrete time Fourier transform.

methods.name: lcapy.sequence.Sequence.DTFT

methods.name: lcapy.sequence.Sequence.plot
documentation:
Plot the sequence.  If `ni` is not specified, it defaults to the
sequence indices.  `ni` can be a vector of specified sequence
indices, a tuple specifing the range, or a constant specifying
the maximum value with the minimum value set to 0.

kwargs include:
axes - the plot axes to use otherwise a new figure is created
xlabel - the x-axis label
ylabel - the y-axis label
xscale - the x-axis scaling, say for plotting as ms
yscale - the y-axis scaling, say for plotting mV
unfilled - the markers are unfilled
markersize - sets the size of the marker
markerfacecolor - sets the marker color
in addition to those supported by the matplotlib plot command.

The plot axes are returned.

methods.name: lcapy.sequence.Sequence.lfilter
documentation:

transfer function is described by a vector `b` of coefficients
for the numerator and an `a` vector of coefficients for the
denominator.

If you would like the response with initial conditions see
`DTfilter.response()`.

For a FIR filter a = [1].

two-port object

+-  -+     +-             -+   +-  -+     +-   -+
| V1 |     | Z11  Z12  Z13 |   | I1 |     | V1z |
| V2 |  =  | Z21  Z22  Z23 |   | I2 |  +  | V2z |
| V3 |     | Z31  Z32  Z33 |   | I3 |     | V3z |
+-  -+     +-             -+   +-  -+     +-   -+

The A, B, G, and H models are invalid for multiports.

Unfortunately, the Z model can blow up for simple networks.

classes.name: lcapy.threeport.Opamp
documentation:
Create an ideal(ish) opamp

```
    |            |          1 --+ +              |                  |                    |          +--- 3
    |        /
    |      /
2 --+ -  /
    |  /
    |/

Each port voltage, Vn, is referenced to a common ground.
Port 1: non-inverting input
Port 2: inverting input
Port 3: output
```

modules.name: lcapy.sym
documentation:
This module contains functions for handling symbols and in particular
for integrating with SymPy.

Copyright 2014--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.sym.symbols_find
documentation: Return list of symbols in arg.  No symbols are cached.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.parse
documentation: Handle arbitrary strings that may refer to multiple symbols.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.sympify1
documentation:
Create a SymPy expression.

The purpose of this function is to head SymPy off at the pass and
apply the defined assumptions.

If `evaluate` is True, the expression is evaluated.

If `override` is True, then create new symbol(s) even if
previously defined by SymPy.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.sympify
documentation:
Create a SymPy expression.

By default, symbols are assumed to be positive reals if no assumptions
are explicitly defined.

Note, this will not modify previously defined symbols with the
same name.  Thus you cannot change the assumptions.

If `evaluate` is True, the expression is evaluated.

If `override` is True, then create new symbol(s) even if
previously defined by SymPy.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.miscsymbol
documentation:
Create a SymPy symbol.

This function allows symbol assumptions to be defined,
e.g., `real=True, integer=True`

By default, symbols are assumed to be positive unless real is
defined.

If `name` is already a symbol, it is overridden unless it is
a domain symbol in which case `force` must be `True`.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.usersymbol
documentation:
Create a SymPy symbol.

This function allows symbol assumptions to be defined,
e.g., `real=True, integer=True`

By default, symbols are assumed to be positive unless real is
defined.

If `name` is already a symbol, it is overridden unless it is
a domain symbol in which case `force` must be `True`.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.domainsymbol
documentation:
Create a SymPy symbol and register as a domain symbol
that should not be overwritten.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.symsimplify
documentation:
Simplify a SymPy expression.  This is a hack to work around
problems with SymPy's simplify API.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.simplify
documentation:
Simplify an Lcapy expression.  This is not straightforward, see
sympy.simplify.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.symdebug
documentation: See also the SymPy function srepr.

functions.name: lcapy.sym.symbol_delete
documentation:
Delete symbol.  This is useful if a symbol needs to be redefined
with different assumptions.

Create an Lcapy difference equation.

This is an Lcapy expression of the form Eq(lhs, rhs).
For example,

The left hand side (lhs) and right hand side subexpressions
