methods.name: lcapy.omegaexpr.AngularFourierDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot angular frequency response at values specified by wvector.

There are many plotting options, see matplotlib.pyplot.plot.

For example:
    V.plot(fvector, log_frequency=True)
    V.real.plot(fvector, color='black')
    V.phase.plot(fvector, color='black', linestyle='--')

By default complex data is plotted as separate plots of magnitude (dB)
and phase.

`kwargs` include:
`axes` - the plot axes to use otherwise a new figure is created
`xlabel` - the x-axis label
`ylabel` - the y-axis label
`ylabel2` - the second y-axis label if needed, say for mag and phase
`xscale` - the x-axis scaling, say for plotting as ms
`yscale` - the y-axis scaling, say for plotting mV
`norm` - use normalized frequency
`dbmin` - the smallest value to plot in dB (default -120)
in addition to those supported by the matplotlib plot command.

The plot axes are returned.  This is a tuple for magnitude/phase or
real/imaginary plots.

methods.name: lcapy.omegaexpr.AngularFourierDomainExpression.bode_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Bode
plot (but without the straight line approximations).  wvector
specifies the angular frequencies.  If it is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets
the frequency limits.   Since a logarithmic frequency scale is used,
f1 must be greater than 0.

`unwrap` controls phase unwrapping (default True).

For more info, see `plot`.

methods.name: lcapy.omegaexpr.AngularFourierDomainExpression.nyquist_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response as a Nyquist plot (imaginary part versus
real part).  wvector specifies the angular frequencies.  If it
is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits as (f1, f2).

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

The unit circle is shown by default.  This can be disabled
with `unitcircle=False`.

methods.name: lcapy.omegaexpr.AngularFourierDomainExpression.nichols_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response as a Nichols plot (dB versus phase).
wvector specifies the angular frequencies.  If it is a tuple
(f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits as (f1, f2).

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

modules.name: lcapy.admittance
documentation:
This module provides admittance support.

Copyright 2019--2024 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.admittance.admittance
documentation:
Create an admittance object from the specified arg.

Y(omega) = G(omega) + j * B(omega)

where G is the conductance and B is the susceptance.

Admittance is the reciprocal of impedance:

Z(omega) = 1 / Y(omega)

For example, given a component, cpt, defined as 'R1 1 2' then

methods.name: lcapy.mnacpts.C._kill
documentation: Kill implicit sources due to initial conditions.
Replace C with open-circuit for DC but this loses branch current through C
def _select(self, kind=None):
if kind != 'dc':
return self._copy()
copts = self.opts.copy()
copts.add('nosim')
return self._netmake_opts(copts) + '\n' + self._netmake_O()

modules.name: lcapy.circuit
documentation:
This module provides circuit analysis using modified nodal analysis
(MNA).

The circuit is described using netlists, similar to SPICE, but with
arbitrary node names (except for the ground node which is labelled 0).
The netlists can be loaded from a file or created at run-time. For
example:

```pycon
>>> from lcapy import Circuit
>>> cct = Circuit('''
V_s fred 0
R_a fred 1
R_b 1 0''')
```

Branch currents and branch voltage differences can be found using the
component name as an attribute, for example,

```pycon
>>> cct.V_s.V.pprint()
>>> cct.R_a.I.pprint()
```

Nodal voltages (with respect to the ground node) can be found using
the node name or number as index, for example,

```pycon
>>> cct['fred'].V.pprint()
>>> cct[1].V.pprint()
```

Copyright 2014--2023 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.circuit.Circuit
documentation:
The Circuit class is used for describing networks using
netlists. Despite the name, it does not require a closed path.

Here's an example of using the Circuit class:

cct = Circuit('''
V1 1 0 V; down
R1 1 2 R; right
C1 2 0_2 C; down
W 0 0_2; right''')

The directions after the semicolon are hints for drawing the
schematic and are ignored for the circuit analysis. The last net
is a wire to make the schematic look nice; it is not needed for
circuit analysis. Indeed the capacitor could be connected
directly to nodes 2 and 0.

The nodes are usually numbers but can be any alphanumeric name
including underscores. By default, nodes with underscores are not
drawn.

The circuit components are also usually numbered but again they
can be any alphanumeric name. They can also have anonymous names,
as for the wire in the example. Internally they are enumerated
sequentially for each component type: W#1, W#2, etc.

The circuit can be displayed using:
\>>> cct.draw()

The schematic can be saved to a file using:
\>>> cct.draw('schematic.pdf')

The transform domain voltages across a component can be found using:
\>>> cct.V1.V

This is found using modified nodal analysis for each type of
independent source in the circuit (AC, DC, transient, noise).
Once this is performed, the results are cached until the network
is modified.

The transform domain currents through a component can be found using:
\>>> cct.R1.I

The transform domain nodal voltages with respect to the ground node (0)
can be found using:
cct[2].V

The time domain voltages and currents are displayed using
lowercase attributes v and i. For example,
\>>> cct.C1.v. This is equivalent to >>> cct.C1.V(t).

The impedance between nodes 2 and 0 can be found using:
\>>> Z = cct.impedance(2, 0)

The open-circuit voltage between nodes 2 and 0 can be found using:
\>>> Z = cct.Voc(2, 0)

The Thevenin equivalent circuit between nodes 2 and 0 can be found
using:
\>>> thevenin = cct.thevenin(2, 0)

The s-domain model can be drawn using:
\>>> cct.s_model().draw()

Create a netlist.

`layout` can be 'horizontal', 'vertical', or 'ladder'.

`evalf` can be False or an integer specifying the number of
decimal places used to evaluate floats.

documentation: Enter the python debugger.

documentation: Convert a Network object into a Schematic object.

methods.name: lcapy.network.Network.draw
documentation:
Draw schematic of network.

filename specifies the name of the file to produce.  If None,
the schematic is displayed on the screen.

Note, if using Jupyter, then need to first issue command %matplotlib inline

`layout` is either 'horizontal', 'vertical', or 'ladder'.

`evalf` can be False or an integer specifying the number of
decimal places used to evaluate floats.

`kwargs` include:
   label_ids: True to show component ids
   label_values: True to display component values
   draw_nodes: True to show all nodes, False to show no nodes,
     'primary' to show primary nodes,
     'connections' to show nodes that connect more than two components,
     'all' to show all nodes
   label_nodes: True to label all nodes, False to label no nodes,
     'primary' to label primary nodes,
     'alpha' to label nodes starting with a letter,
     'pins' to label nodes that are pins on a chip,
     'all' to label all nodes
   style: 'american', 'british', or 'european'
   scale: schematic scale factor, default 1.0
   node_spacing: spacing between component nodes, default 2.0
   cpt_size: size of a component, default 1.5
   dpi: dots per inch for png files
   help_lines: distance between lines in grid, default 0.0 (disabled)
   debug: True to display debug information

transformation is performed using network synthesis of the
network's impedance (note, this ignores the sources).  `form`
includes: cauerI, cauerII, fosterI, fosterII.

Note some methods generate networks with negative value
components.

combination of a resistance and a noise voltage source.

modules.name: lcapy.polyphase
documentation:
This module provides preliminary support for polyphase systems.

Copyright 2020 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.polyphase.polyphase_decompose_matrix
documentation:
Matrix to decompose vector of phase components into the symmetrical
sequence components.  The matrix dimension is `N` x `N`.  This
matrix is equivalent to IDFTmatrix.  The transformation is only
valid for positive frequency components; the complex conjugate is
required for negative frequency components.

functions.name: lcapy.polyphase.polyphase_compose_matrix
documentation:
Matrix to compose symmetrical sequence components into a vector of
phase components.  The matrix dimension is `N` x `N`.  This matrix
is equivalent to DFTmatrix.  The transformation is only valid for
positive frequency components; the complex conjugate is required
for negative frequency components.

modules.name: lcapy.parser
documentation:
This module performs parsing of SPICE-like netlists.  It uses a
custom parser rather than lex/yacc to give better error messages.

Copyright 2015--2023 Michael Hayes, UCECE

functions.name: lcapy.parser.split
documentation:
Split string by specified delimiters but not if a delimiter is
within curly brackets {} or "".
Could use a script to generate parser and parsing tables if speed
was important.
Each line of a netlist (called net here) has a name followed by a
number of required parameters (params), a number of optional
parameters, an optional semicolon, and optional comma separated
key-value pairs.
Each param can be a node, keyword, name, or value.
Here's an example: I1 4 5 {Piecewise((V/L, t >= 0))}; right
The name is I1; 4, 5 are nodes; the expression is brackets is a value.
The name or value params are referred to as args.

grammar is a module defining the syntax of a netlist

modules.name: lcapy.seqexpr
documentation:
This module provides the SequenceExpression class to provide
common methods for the discrete-time and discrete-frequency
expressions.

Copyright 2020--2022 Michael Hayes, UCECE

classes.name: lcapy.seqexpr.SequenceExpression

methods.name: lcapy.seqexpr.SequenceExpression.seq
documentation:
Create a Sequence.

```pycon
>>> a = x.seq()
```

The sequence indices are specified with the optional `ni` argument.
For example:

```pycon
>>> a = x.seq(ni=(-1, 0, 1, 2))
```

If the `ni` argument is not specified, the sequence indices
are enumerated from 0.

The sequence indices can be found using the `n` attribute.
This returns a list.

```pycon
>>> a = x.seq().n
[-1, 0, 1, 2]
```

methods.name: lcapy.seqexpr.SequenceExpression.is_stable
documentation:
Return True if all the poles of the signal's z-transform
are within the unit circle.

See also is_marginally_stable.

methods.name: lcapy.seqexpr.SequenceExpression.is_marginally_stable
documentation:
Return True if all the poles of the signal's z-transform
are within or on the unit circle.

See also is_stable.
Superclass of discrete-time and discrete-frequency expressions.

methods.name: lcapy.fexpr.FourierDomainExpression.plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response at values specified by `fvector`.

If `fvector` is a tuple, this sets the frequency limits.

`plot_type` - 'dB-phase', 'dB-phase-degrees', 'mag-phase',
'mag-phase-degrees', 'real-imag', 'mag', 'phase',
'phase-degrees', 'real', or 'imag'.

The default `plot_type` for complex data is `dB-phase`.

`kwargs` include:
`axes` - the plot axes to use otherwise a new figure is created
`xlabel` - the x-axis label
`ylabel` - the y-axis label
`ylabel2` - the second y-axis label if needed, say for mag and phase
`xscale` - the x-axis scaling, say for plotting as ms
`yscale` - the y-axis scaling, say for plotting mV
`norm` - use normalized frequency
`dbmin` - the smallest value to plot in dB (default -120)
`plot_deltas` - plot Dirac deltas as arrows
in addition to those supported by the matplotlib plot command.

The plot axes are returned.  This is a tuple for magnitude/phase or
real/imaginary plots.

There are many plotting options, see lcapy.plot and
matplotlib.pyplot.plot.

For example:
    V.plot(fvector, log_frequency=True)
    V.real.plot(fvector, color='black')
    V.phase.plot(fvector, color='black', linestyle='--')

By default complex data is plotted as separate plots of
magnitude (dB) and phase.

methods.name: lcapy.fexpr.FourierDomainExpression.bode_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response for a frequency-domain phasor as a Bode
plot (but without the straight line approximations).  fvector
specifies the frequencies.  If it is a tuple (f1, f2), it sets
the frequency limits.   Since a logarithmic frequency scale is used,
f1 must be greater than 0.

`unwrap` controls phase unwrapping (default True).

For more info, see `plot`.

methods.name: lcapy.fexpr.FourierDomainExpression.nyquist_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response as a Nyquist plot (imaginary part versus
real part).  fvector specifies the frequencies.  If it is
a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits as (f1, f2).

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

The unit circle is shown by default.  This can be disabled with `unitcircle=False`.

methods.name: lcapy.fexpr.FourierDomainExpression.nichols_plot
documentation:
Plot frequency response as a Nichols plot (dB part versus
phase).  fvector specifies the frequencies.  If it is
a tuple (f1, f2), it sets the frequency limits as (f1, f2).

`npoints` set the number of plotted points.

methods.name: lcapy.fexpr.FourierDomainExpression.hilbert
documentation: Attempt Hilbert transform. This is an alias for HT.

methods.name: lcapy.fexpr.FourierDomainExpression.HT
documentation: Attempt Hilbert transform.

methods.name: lcapy.fexpr.FourierDomainExpression.inverse_hilbert
documentation: Attempt Hilbert transform. This is an alias for IHT.

methods.name: lcapy.fexpr.FourierDomainExpression.IHT
documentation: Attempt Hilbert transform.
Fourier domain expression or symbol.

modules.name: lcapy.superpositionvoltage
documentation:
This module provides the SuperpositionVoltage class.  This
represents voltages as a superposition in different transform domains.

For example, the following expression is a superposition of a DC
component, an AC component, and a transient component:

V1 = SuperpositionVoltage('1 + 2 * cos(2 * pi * 3 * t) + 3 * u(t)')

V1(t) returns the time domain expression
V1(s) returns the Laplace domain expression
V1(omega) returns the Fourier domain expression with angular frequency
V1(f) returns the Fourier domain expression with linear frequency

V1.dc returns the DC component
V1.ac returns a dictionary of the AC components, keyed by the frequency
V1.transient returns the time-domain transient component

V1.is_dc returns True if a pure DC signal
V1.is_ac returns True if a pure AC signal
V1.is_transient returns True if a pure transient signal
V1.has_dc returns True if has a DC signal
V1.has_ac returns True if has an AC signal
V1.has_transient returns True if has a transient signal

Copyright 2019--2023 Michael Hayes, UCECE

modules.name: lcapy.abc
documentation:
This module exports all latin and greek letters as Symbols, so you can
conveniently do

    >>> from lcapy.abc import x, y

instead of the slightly more clunky-looking

    >>> from lcapy import symbols
    >>> x, y = symbols('x y')

Unlike sympy.abc, this does not define symbols that clash with Lcapy's
domain variables.

modules.name: lcapy.twoport
documentation:
This module supports simple linear two-port networks.

Copyright 2014--2023 Michael Hayes, UCECE

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.TwoPortMixin.is_buffered
documentation:
Return true if two-port is buffered, i.e., any load
on the output has no affect on the input.

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.TwoPortMixin.is_bilateral
documentation: Return true if two-port is bilateral.

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.TwoPortMixin.is_reciprocal
documentation: Return true if two-port is reciprocal.

documentation: Return true if two-port is symmetrical.

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.TwoPortMixin.is_series
documentation: Return true if two-port is a series network.

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.TwoPortMixin.is_shunt
documentation: Return true if two-port is a shunt network.

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.TwoPortMixin.voltage_gain
documentation:
Return V2 / V1 for I2 = 0 with internal sources zero.

This is an alias for forward_voltage_gain.

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.AMatrix.transformer
documentation: The voltage gain alpha = 1 / a, where a is the turns ratio.

A-parameters (ABCD parameters, chain matrix)

```
+-  -+     +-       -+   +-  -+
| V1 |  =  | A11  A12|   | V2 |
| I1 |     | A21  A22|   |-I2 |
+-  -+     +-       -+   +-  -+

       +-         -+
units  | 1     ohm |
       | 1/ohm   1 |
       +-         -+
```

A buffered two-port has A12 = A22 = 0.

A = inv(B)

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.BMatrix.voltage_amplifier
documentation:
Voltage amplifier
Af forward voltage gain
Ar reverse voltage gain (ideally 0)
Yin input admittance (ideally 0)
Zout output impedance (ideally 0)

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.BMatrix.current_amplifier
documentation:
Current amplifier
Af forward current gain
Ar reverse current gain (ideally 0)
Yin input admittance (ideally 0)
Yout output impedance (ideally 0)

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.BMatrix.transformer
documentation: The voltage gain alpha = 1 / a, where a is the turns ratio.

B-parameters (inverse ABCD parameters)

```
+-  -+     +-       -+   +-  -+
| V2 |  =  | B11  B12|   | V1 |
|-I2 |     | B21  B22|   | I1 |
+-  -+     +-       -+   +-  -+

       +-         -+
units  | 1     ohm |
       | 1/ohm   1 |
       +-         -+
```

B = inv(A)

classes.name: lcapy.twoport.GMatrix
documentation:
G-parameters (inverse hybrid parameters)

::

: +- -+ +- -+ +- -+
  | V2 | = | G11 G12| | I2 |
  | I1 | | G21 G22| | V1 |
  +- -+ +- -+ +- -+

  > +- -+

  units | ohm 1 |

  : 1 1/ohm |

    +- -+

G = inv(H)

classes.name: lcapy.twoport.SMatrix
documentation:
S-parameters (scattering parameters)

```
+-  -+     +-       -+   +-  -+
| b1 |  =  | S11  S12|   | a1 |
| b2 |     | S21  S22|   | a2 |
+-  -+     +-       -+   +-  -+
```

Each element in the s-matrix has units of impedance.

classes.name: lcapy.twoport.TMatrix

methods.name: lcapy.twoport.TMatrix.equation
documentation: Note, another convention uses a1, b1 in terms of b2, a2.

T-parameters (scattering transfer parameters)

```
+-  -+     +-       -+   +-  -+
| b1 |  =  | T11  T12|   | a2 |
| a2 |     | T21  T22|   | b2 |
+-  -+     +-       -+   +-  -+
```

```
units  | 1/ohm 1/ohm |
```

Y = inv(Z)

```
---------------

  +---------+
--+   OP    +--
```

is drawn differently.

classes.name: lcapy.twoport.SeriesPair
documentation:
Two-port comprising a pair of one-ports in series configuration

```
  +---------+
--+   OP1   +--

--+   OP2   +--
  +---------+
```

Note, this has a singular Y matrix. Electrically it is equivalent
to a series combination of OP1 and OP2, but it is useful for drawing
OP1 aligned with OP2.

```
   |   |
   +-+-+
     |
-----+-----
```

Note, this has a singular Z matrix.

classes.name: lcapy.twoport.Transformer
documentation:
Transformer voltage gain alpha, current gain 1 / alpha.
Note, alpha = 1 / a where a is the turns ratio defined as the
number of primary turns to the number of secondary turns, a = N_2 / N_1.

Unlike with IdealTransformer, the parameter alpha can be a function of s.

classes.name: lcapy.twoport.IdealTransformer
documentation:
Ideal transformer voltage gain alpha, current gain 1 / alpha.
Note, alpha = 1 / a where a is the turns ratio defined as the
number of primary turns to the number of secondary turns, a = N_2 / N_1.

alpha must be a constant, otherwise use Transformer if the
parameter alpha can be a function of s.

documentation:
Ideal gyrator with gyration resistance R.

A gyrator converts a voltage to current and a current to voltage.
Cascaded gyrators act like a transformer

modules.name: lcapy.expr
documentation:
This module provides the Expr class.  This attempts to create a
consistent interface to SymPy's expressions.

Copyright 2014--2024 Michael Hayes, UCECE
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: lcapy.expr.expr
documentation:
Create Lcapy expression from arg.

If `arg` is an `Expr` it is returned, unless `assumptions` is specified.

If `arg` is a string:
   If a t symbol is found in the string a TimeDomainExpression object is created.
   If a s symbol is found in the string a LaplaceDomainExpression object is created.
   If a f symbol is found in the string an FourierDomainExpression object is created.
   If an omega symbol is found in the string an AngularFourierDomainExpression object is created.

For example, v = expr('3 * exp(-t / tau) * u(t)')

V = expr('5 * s', causal=True)

If `override` is True, then create new symbol(s) even if
previously defined by SymPy.

functions.name: lcapy.expr.equation
documentation:
Create an Lcapy equation.

This is an Lcapy expression of the form Eq(lhs, rhs).
For example,
e = equation('Y(s)', 'X(s) * 2 * s')

The left hand side(lhs) and right hand side subexpressions
can be obtained with the `lhs` and `rhs` attributes.

functions.name: lcapy.expr.symbol
documentation:
Create Lcapy symbols from whitespace or comma delimited string of
symbol names.

By default, symbols are assumed to be positive and real.

`symbols('a b', real=True)` creates real symbols

`symbols('a b', complex=True)` creates complex symbols

`symbols('a b', integer=True, positive=True)` creates positive integer symbols

See also symbols.

functions.name: lcapy.expr.symbols
documentation:
Create Lcapy symbols from whitespace or comma delimited string of
symbol names.  See also symbol.

documentation: Set units to radians.  See also radians() that converts degrees to radians.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprDict.subs
documentation:
Substitute symbols in the expression.

`args` is either:
- two arguments, e.g. foo.subs(old, new)
- one iterable argument, e.g. foo.subs(iterable). The iterable may be
   o an iterable container with (old, new) pairs. In this case the
     replacements are processed in the order given with successive
     patterns possibly affecting replacements already made.
   o a dict or set whose key/value items correspond to old/new pairs.

The domain of the result can be coerced using `domain` argument.
Facade class for dictionary created by sympy.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprList.subs
documentation:
Substitute symbols for each expression in list.

`args` is either:
- two arguments, e.g. foo.subs(old, new)
- one iterable argument, e.g. foo.subs(iterable). The iterable may be
   o an iterable container with (old, new) pairs. In this case the
     replacements are processed in the order given with successive
     patterns possibly affecting replacements already made.
   o a dict or set whose key/value items correspond to old/new pairs.

The domain of the result can be coerced using `domain` argument.

Facade class for list created by sympy.

classes.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.__new__
documentation: Tuples are immutable, need to use __new__

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.__call__
documentation: Perform substitution/transformation on each element.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.subs
documentation:
Substitute symbols for each expression in tuple.

`args` is either:
- two arguments, e.g. foo.subs(old, new)
- one iterable argument, e.g. foo.subs(iterable). The iterable may be
   o an iterable container with (old, new) pairs. In this case the
     replacements are processed in the order given with successive
     patterns possibly affecting replacements already made.
   o a dict or set whose key/value items correspond to old/new pairs.

The domain of the result can be coerced using `domain` argument.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.has
documentation:
Test whether any subexpressions matches any of the patterns.  For example,
V.has(exp(t))
V.has(t)

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.remove_undefs
documentation:
Remove applied undefined functions (undefs) with symbols.
For example, `V(s)` is replaced with `V`.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.solve
documentation:
Solve system of equations, and return as ExprDict.
See sympy.solve for usage.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.fval
documentation: Evaluate expression and return as a tuple of python float values.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.cval
documentation: Evaluate expression and return as a tuple of python complex values.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.ExprTuple.value
documentation: Evaluate expression and return as a list of python int, float, or complex values.
Facade class for tuple created by sympy.

classes.name: lcapy.expr.Expr

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr._pexpr
documentation: Return expression for printing.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__init__
documentation:
There are three types of assumptions:
  1. The sympy assumptions associated with symbols, for example,
     real=True.
  2. The expr assumptions such as dc, ac, causal.  These are primarily
     to help the inverse Laplace transform for LaplaceDomainExpression classes.
  3. Additional parameters such as nid for NoiseDomain and omega
     for PhasorDomain.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__str__
documentation: String representation of expression.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__repr__
documentation:
This is called by repr(expr).  It is used, e.g., when printing
in the debugger.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr._repr_pretty_
documentation:
This is used by jupyter notebooks to display an expression using
unicode.  It is also called by IPython when displaying an
expression.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr._repr_latex_
documentation:
This is used by jupyter notebooks to display an expression using
LaTeX markup.  However, this requires mathjax.  If this method
is not defined, jupyter falls back on _repr_pretty_ which
outputs unicode.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr._latex
documentation:
Make latex string.  This is called by sympy.latex when it
encounters an Expr type.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr._pretty
documentation: Make pretty string.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.canonical_units
documentation:
Return the canonical units of the expression.  This is a simplified
form, so volt * ampere becomes watt.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.units
documentation: Return the units of the expression.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.units
documentation: Set the units of the expression; these are simplified into canonical form.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_causal
documentation: Return True if zero for t < 0.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_unknown
documentation: Return True if behaviour is unknown for t < 0.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_complex_signal
documentation: Return True if time-domain signal is complex.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_conditional
documentation: Return True if expression has a condition, such as t >= 0.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_rational_function
documentation: Return True if expression is a rational function.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_strictly_proper
documentation:
Return True if the degree of the dominator is greater
than the degree of the numerator.
This will throw an exception if the expression is not a
rational function.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.ac
documentation: Return the AC components.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.dc
documentation: Return the DC component.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.transient
documentation: Return the transient component.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.fval
documentation:
Evaluate expression and return as a python float value.
Small imaginary components are discarded.

Use `cval` to return a complex value.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.cval
documentation: Evaluate expression and return as a python complex value.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.val
documentation:
Return floating point value of expression if it can be evaluated,
otherwise the expression.

This is equivalant to `evalf` and returns an Lcapy Expr
object.  If you want a Python numerical value use expr.fval
for a float value or expr.cval for a complex value.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.value
documentation:
Return value that can be represented as a Python int, float, or
complex object.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.evalf
documentation:
Convert constants in an expression to floats, evaluated to `n`
decimal places.  If the expression is a constant, return the
floating point result.

This returns an Lcapy Expr object.   If you want a numerical value
use expr.fval for a float value or expr.cval for a complex value.

See sympy.evalf for more details.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__getattr__
documentation: This will allow sym.sympify to magically extract the sympy expression
but it will also bypass our __rmul__, __radd__, etc. methods that get called
when sympy punts.  Thus pi * t becomes a Mul rather than TimeDomainExpression.
def _sympy_(self):
# This is called from sym.sympify
return self.expr

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.debug
documentation:
Print the SymPy expression and the assumptions for all symbols in
the expression.  See also srepr.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.srepr
documentation: Print the SymPy abstract syntax tree for the expression.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.sympy
documentation: Return SymPy expression.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.expr_with_units
documentation: Return SymPy expression with units.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.expr_with_canonical_units
documentation: Return SymPy expression with canonical units.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.func
documentation:
Return the top-level function in the Sympy Expression.

For example, this returns Mul for the expression `3 * s`.
See also args to return the args, in this case `(3, s)`

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__abs__
documentation: Absolute value.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__neg__
documentation: Negation.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__mul__
documentation: Multiply.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__rmul__
documentation: Reverse multiply.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__truediv__
documentation: True divide.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__rtruediv__
documentation: Reverse true divide.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__floordiv__
documentation: Floor divide.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__rfloordiv__
documentation: Floor divide.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__add__
documentation: Add.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__radd__
documentation: Reverse add.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__sub__
documentation: Subtract.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__rsub__
documentation: Reverse subtract.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__pow__
documentation: Power.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__rpow__
documentation: Reverse pow, x**self.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__or__
documentation: Parallel combination.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__eq__
documentation:
Test for mathematical equality as far as possible.
This cannot be guaranteed since it depends on simplification.
Note, SymPy comparison is for structural equality.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__ne__
documentation:
Test for mathematical inequality as far as possible.
This cannot be guaranteed since it depends on simplification.
Note, SymPy comparison is for structural equality.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__gt__
documentation: Greater than.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__ge__
documentation: Greater than or equal.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__lt__
documentation: Less than.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__le__
documentation: Less than or equal.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.cancel_terms
documentation:
Simplify terms in expression individually by converting
each to rational functions.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.convolve
documentation:
Convolve self with x.

y(t) = int_{taumin}^{taumax} self(tau) x(t - tau) d tau

If `commutate` is True, swap order of functions in integral.

The result is an unevaluated integral.  It can be evaluated using
the `doit()` method.

Note, this method not simplify the convolution integral if one
of the functions contains a Dirac delta.  This can be done
calling the `simplify_dirac_delta()` method followed by the
`simplify()` method.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.parallel
documentation: Parallel combination.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.copy
documentation: Copy the expression.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.conj
documentation: Return complex conjugate.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.conjugate
documentation: Return complex conjugate.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.real
documentation:
Return real part.

 Note, SymPy does not always extract the real part.  For example,
`exp(t*(-1 - sqrt(5)*j))/(20*sqrt(5) - 20*j)` or
`exp(j * f) / (1 - exp(j * f))`

A work-around is to use `rationalize_denominator()` or
`expand(complex=True)` first.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.re
documentation: Return real part, see `real`

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.imag
documentation:
Return imaginary part.

Note, SymPy does not always extract the imaginary part.  For example,
`exp(t*(-1 - sqrt(5)*j))/(20*sqrt(5) - 20*j)` or
`exp(j * f) / (1 - exp(j * f))`

A work-around is to use `rationalize_denominator()` or
`expand(complex=True)` first.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.im
documentation: Return imaginary part, see `im`

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.real_imag
documentation: Rewrite as x + j * y.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.ba
documentation: Return lists of numerator and denominator coefficients.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.a
documentation: Return list of denominator coefficients.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.b
documentation: Return list of numerator coefficients.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.K
documentation: Return gain.  Note, this removes the units.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.N
documentation:
Return numerator of rational function.
The denominator is chosen so that it is a polynomial.
Note, this removes the units.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.D
documentation:
Return denominator of rational function.
The denominator is chosen so that it is a polynomial.
Note, this removes the units.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.numerator
documentation:
Return numerator of rational function.
The denominator is chosen so that it is a polynomial.
Note, this removes the units.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.denominator
documentation:
Return denominator of rational function.
The denominator is chosen so that it is a polynomial.
Note, this removes the units.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.rationalize_denominator
documentation:
Rationalize denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by
complex conjugate of denominator.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.divide_top_and_bottom
documentation: Divide numerator and denominator by common factor.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.multiply_top_and_bottom
documentation: Multiply numerator and denominator by common factor.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.magnitude
documentation: Return magnitude.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.abs
documentation: Return magnitude.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.sign
documentation: Return sign.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.dB
documentation:
Return magnitude in dB.  If squared voltage, squared current,
or power, this uses 10 * log10 otherwise 20 * log10.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.phase
documentation: Return phase in radians.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.phase_degrees
documentation: Return phase in degrees.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.angle
documentation: Return phase angle (in radians).

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.polar
documentation: Return in polar format.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.cartesian
documentation: Return in Cartesian format.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_number
documentation: Returns True if expression is a number.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_constant
documentation: Returns True if expression does not have any free symbols (compare with `is_unchanging`).

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_realizable
documentation:
Return True if the signal's Laplace transform = 0
   in limit as s -> oo.  This is equivalent to the Laplace
   transform being strictly proper.

Note, with this definition `cos(t)` is considered realizable.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_stable
documentation:
Return True if all the poles of the signal's Laplace transform
are in the LH plane.

Note, `exp(-alpha * t)` is considered stable since the
unilateral Laplace transform ignores the region where `t < 0`.

See also is_marginally_stable.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_marginally_stable
documentation:
Return True if all the poles of the signal's Laplace transform
are in the LH plane or on the boundary.

Note, `exp(-alpha * t)` is considered stable since the
unilateral Laplace transform ignores the region where `t < 0`.

See also is_stable.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.is_unchanging
documentation: Returns True if expression does not have a domain variable (compare with `is_constant`).

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.evaluate
documentation:
Evaluate expression at arg.  `arg` may be a scalar or a vector.
The result is of type float or complex.

If arg is iterable, a NumPy array is returned.

There can be only one or fewer undefined variables in the expression.
This is replaced by `arg` and then evaluated to obtain a result.

See also `fval` and `cval`.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.has
documentation:
Test whether any subexpressions matches any of the patterns.  For example,
V.has(exp(t))
V.has(t)

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.has_symbol
documentation:
Test if have symbol contained.  For example,
V.has_symbol('a')
V.has_symbol(t)

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.transform
documentation:
Transform into a different domain.

If arg is f, s, t, omega, jomega perform domain transformation,
otherwise perform substitution.

Note (5 * s)(omega) will fail since 5 * s is assumed not to be
causal and so Fourier transform is unknown.  However, Zs(5 *
s)(omega) will work since Zs is assumed to be causal.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.__call__
documentation:
Transform domain or substitute arg for variable.

Substitution is performed if arg is a tuple, list, numpy
array, or constant.  If arg is a tuple or list return a list.
If arg is an numpy array, return numpy array.

Domain transformation is performed if arg is a domain variable
or an expression of a domain variable.

See also evaluate.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.limit
documentation:
Determine limit of expression(var) at var = value.
If `dir == '+'` search from right else if `dir == '-'`
search from left.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.separate_dirac_delta
documentation: Separate Dirac delta terms from expression.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify
documentation:
Simplify expression.

This throws the kitchen sink at the problem but can be slow.

The result is cached so the second call in `expr.simplify().simplify()`
has no effect.  Use `expr.simplify().simplify(force=True)` to
force a second round of simplification.

See also simplify_terms and simplify_factors.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_units
documentation: Simplify units into canonical form.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_conjugates
documentation: Combine complex conjugate terms.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_factors
documentation: Simplify factors in expression individually.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_terms
documentation: Simplify terms in expression individually.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_sin_cos
documentation: Simplify c * cos(theta) - s * sin(theta) as A * cos(theta - phi).

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_dirac_delta
documentation:
Simplify DiracDelta(4 * t + 2) to DiracDelta(t + 0.5) / 4
and DiracDelta(t) * x(t) to DiracDelta * x(0).

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_heaviside
documentation:
Simplify Heaviside(4 * t + 2) to Heaviside(t + 0.5)
and Heaviside(t)**2 to Heaviside(t), etc.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_unit_impulse
documentation: Simplify UnitImpulse(4 * k + 8) to UnitImpulse(k + 2), etc.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.simplify_rect
documentation:
Simplify rect(4 * t + 2) to rect(t + 0.5)
and rect(t)**2 to rect(t), etc.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.expand_hyperbolic_trig
documentation: Convert cosh(x) to exp(x) + exp(-x), etc.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.subs
documentation:
Substitute symbols in expression.

`args` is either:
- two arguments, e.g. foo.subs(old, new)
- one iterable argument, e.g. foo.subs(iterable). The iterable may be
   o an iterable container with (old, new) pairs. In this case the
     replacements are processed in the order given with successive
     patterns possibly affecting replacements already made.
   o a dict or set whose key/value items correspond to old/new pairs.

The domain of the result can be coerced using `domain` argument.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.differentiate
documentation: Differentiate expression.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.doit
documentation: Evaluate unevaluated functions such as integrals and sums.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.drop_dt
documentation: Replace V(n * dt) with V(n)

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.next_timestep
documentation: Replace n with n + 1

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.integrate
documentation:
Integrate expression.

For example `exp(-3 * t).integrate((t, 0, oo))` gives `1 / 3`.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.rewrite
documentation:
Rewrite expression.

For example, `exp(j*a*t).rewrite(cos)` gives `ⅉ⋅sin(4⋅t) +
cos(4⋅t)`.  Similarly, `cos(2 * t).rewrite(exp)` will expand
the cosine as two complex exponentials.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.solve
documentation:
Solve expression.  This returns a list of solutions.  An empty list
is returned if no solutions are found.  Note, by default,
Lcapy assumes symbols are positive so an innocuous expression may fail
to give a result if the solution is negative.

For example:
`>>> x = symbols('x')
 >>> y = x + 3
 >>> y.solve(x)
 []
 >>> x = symbols('x', positive=False)
 >>> y = x + 3
 >>> y.solve(x)
 [-3]`

If no symbols are specified, all free symbols are solved for.

See also `nsolve` for a numerical solver.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.nsolve
documentation:
Solve expression numerically.  This returns a list of solutions.
An empty list is returned if no solutions are found.

`x0` is starting point close to the solution.

See sympy.solvers.solvers.nsolve for details.

See also `solve` for a symbolic solver.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.split_dirac_delta
documentation:
Return expression as a list of terms.  The first term has no
DiracDeltas, the second term collates the DiracDeltas, the
third term collates derivatives of DiracDeltas, etc.

For example, u(t) + DiractDelta(t, 1) returns
[u(t), 0, DiracDelta(t, 1)]

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.symbols
documentation: Return dictionary of symbols in the expression keyed by name.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.roots
documentation:
Return roots of expression as a dictionary.  Note this may not find
them all.  In particular, if the rational function has a
degree of five or higher.

If `pairs` is True, return two dictionaries.  The first
contains the conjugate pairs and the second contains the
others

If `aslist` is True, return roots as list.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.zeros
documentation:
Return zeros of expression as a dictionary Note this may not find
them all.  In particular, if the denominator of the rational
function has a degree of five or higher.

Zeros at infinity are not returned.  There will be q - p zeros
at infinity for a rational function with a numerator of degree p
and a denominator of degree q.

If `pairs` is True, return two dictionaries.  The first
contains the conjugate pairs and the second contains the
others

If `aslist` is True, return zeros as list.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.poles
documentation:
Return poles of expression as a dictionary.  Note this may not find
them all.  In particular, if the denominator of the rational
function has a degree of five or higher.

Poles at infinity are not returned.  There will be p - q poles
at infinity for a rational function with a numerator of degree p
and a denominator of degree q.

`damping` can be None, 'over', 'critical', or 'under'.  This
is used to make assumptions about quadratic poles of the form
`a + sqrt(b - c)`.  For example, with the 'under' assumption,
this gets changed to `a + j sqrt(c - b)`, and with the
'critical' assumption, this gets changed to `a`.

If `pairs` is True, return two dictionaries.  The first
contains the conjugate pairs and the second contains the
others.

If `aslist` is True, return poles as list.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.parameterize
documentation:
Parameterize first and second-order expressions.

For example, pexpr, defs = expr.parameterize()

If parameterization is successful, defs is a dictionary
of the parameters.  The original expression can be obtained
with pexpr.subs(defs)

For first order systems, parameterize as:

K * (s + beta) / (s + alpha)

K / (s + alpha)

K (s + beta)

where appropriate.

If `zeta` is True, parameterize second-order expression in
standard form using damping factor and natural frequency
representation, i.e.

N(s) / (s**2 + 2 * zeta * omega_0 * s + omega_0**2)

otherwise parameterize as

N(s) / (s**2 + 2 * sigma_1 * s + omega_1**2 + sigma_1**2)

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.canonical
documentation:
Convert rational function to canonical form (aka polynomial form);
this is like general form but with a unity highest power of
denominator.  For example,

(5 * s**2 + 5 * s + 5) / (s**2 + 4)

If factor_const is True, factor constants from numerator, for example,

5 * (s**2 + s + 1) / (s**2 + 4)

This is also called gain-polynomial form.

See also general, partfrac, standard, timeconst, and ZPK

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.general
documentation:
Convert rational function to general form.  For example,

(5 * s**2 + 10 * s + 5) / (s**2 + 4)

See also canonical, partfrac, standard, timeconst, and ZPK.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.partfrac
documentation:
Convert rational function into partial fraction form.   For example,

5 + (5 - 15 * j / 4) / (s + 2 * j) + (5 + 15 * j / 4) / (s - 2 * j)

If `combine_conjugates` or `pairs` is True then pairs of partial
fractions for complex conjugate poles are combined.   This creates
a sum of biquad sections.

`method` can be 'sub' (substitution method, the default) or
'ec' (equating cofficients method).

`damping` can be None, 'over', 'critical', or 'under'.  This
is used to make assumptions about quadratic poles of the form
`a + sqrt(b - c)`.  For example, with the 'under' assumption,
this gets changed to `a + j sqrt(c - b)`, and with the
'critical' assumption, this gets changed to `a`.

See also canonical, standard, general, timeconst, and ZPK.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.recippartfrac
documentation:
Convert rational function into partial fraction form
using reciprocal of variable.

For example, if H = 5 * (s**2 + 1) / (s**2 + 5*s + 4)
then H.recippartfrac() gives
5/4 - 10/(3*(1 + 1/s)) + 85/(48*(1/4 + 1/s))

If combine_conjugates is True then the pair of partial
fractions for complex conjugate poles are combined.

`damping` can be None, 'over', 'critical', or 'under'.  This
is used to make assumptions about quadratic poles of the form
`a + sqrt(b - c)`.  For example, with the 'under' assumption,
this gets changed to `a + j sqrt(c - b)`, and with the
'critical' assumption, this gets changed to `a`.

`method` can be 'sub' (substitution method, the default) or
'ec' (equating cofficients method).

See also canonical, standard, general, partfrac, timeconst, and ZPK.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.standard
documentation:
Convert rational function into mixed fraction form.  For example,

(5 * s - 5) / (s**2 + 4) + 5

This is the sum of strictly proper rational function and a
polynomial.

See also canonical, general, partfrac, timeconst, and ZPK.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.mixedfrac
documentation: This is an alias for standard and may be deprecated.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.timeconst
documentation:
Convert rational function into time constant form.  For example,

5 * (s**2 + 2 * s + 1) / (4 * (s**2 / 4 + 1))

See also timeconst_terms, canonical, general, standard,
partfrac and ZPK.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.timeconst_terms
documentation: Convert each term of expression into time constant form.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.ZPK
documentation:
Convert to zero-pole-gain (ZPK) form (factored form).  For example,

5 * (s + 1)**2 / ((s - 2 * j) * (s + 2 * j))

If `combine_conjugates` or `pairs` is True, then conjugate pairs are
combined to create a product of biquad sections.  For example,

5 * (s + 1)**2 / (s**2 + 4)

Note, both the numerator and denominator are expressed as
products of monic factors, i.e., (s + 1 / 3) rather than (3 * s + 1).

See also canonical, general, standard, partfrac, and timeconst.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.factored
documentation:
Convert to factored form.  For example,

5 * (s + 1)**2 / ((s - 2 * j) * (s + 2 * j))

If `pairs` is True, then conjugate pairs are combined.  For example,

5 * (s + 1)**2 / (s**2 + 4)

This is an alias for ZPK.  See also canonical, general,
standard, partfrac, and timeconst.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.expandcanonical
documentation:
Expand in terms for different powers with each term
expressed in canonical form.  For example,

s / (s**2 + 4) + 5 / (s**2 + 4)

See also canonical, general, partfrac, timeconst, and ZPK.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.expand_functions
documentation:
Expand functions in expression.

For example `rect(t)` -> `u(t + 1 / 2) - u(t - 1 / 2)`

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.expand_response
documentation:
Expand expression into a sum of Lcapy responses, where each
response has the form B(var) * expp(-var * T) / A(var).
This helps with inverse Laplace transforms.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.coeffs
documentation:
Return list of coeffs assuming the expr is a polynomial in terms of
`var`.  If `var` is None, the default variable is used.
The highest powers come first.

This will fail for a rational function.  Instead use
expr.N.coeffs or expr.D.coeffs for numerator or denominator
respectively.

If `norm` is True, normalize coefficients so highest power is 1.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.normcoeffs
documentation:
Return list of coeffs (normalized so the highest power is 1)
assuming the expr is a polynomial in s.  The highest powers
come first.  This will fail for a rational function.  Instead
use expr.N.normcoeffs or expr.D.normcoeffs for numerator or
denominator respectively.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.degree
documentation:
Return the degree (order) of the rational function.

This the maximum of the numerator and denominator degrees.
Note zero has a degree of -inf.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.Ndegree
documentation:
Return the degree (order) of the numerator of a rational function.
This will throw an exception if the expression is not a
rational function.

Note zero has a degree of -inf.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.Ddegree
documentation:
Return the degree (order) of the denominator of a rational function.
This will throw an exception if the expression is not a
rational function.

Note zero has a degree of -inf.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.prune_HOT
documentation:
Prune higher order terms if expression is a polynomial
so that resultant approximate expression has the desired degree.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.ratfloat
documentation:
This converts rational numbers in an expression to floats.
See also floatrat.

For example, t / 5 -> 0.2 * t

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.floatrat
documentation:
This converts floating point numbers to rational numbers in an
expression.  See also ratfloat.

For example, 0.2 * t - > t / 5

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_dominant
documentation:
Approximate expression using ball-park numerical values for the
symbols to decide which terms in a sum dominate the sum.  A term
is neglected if its absolute value is below `threshold` times the
maximum absolute value of the terms in the sum.

`defs` is a dict, set, list, or tuple of symbol definitions;
see `subs()`.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_exp
documentation:
Approximate exp(a) with a Pade approximant order [ndegree,
ddegree].

The best time-domain response (without a jump) is achieved
with 'ndegree == ddegree - 1', where `numer` is the degree of
the numerator.  The best frequency-domain response is achieved
with `ndegree == ddegree`.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_fractional_power
documentation:
Approximate s**a, where a is fractional, with a Pade approximant of
order [ndegree, ddegree].

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_hyperbolic_trig
documentation:
Approximate cosh(a), sinh(a), tanh(a) with a Pade approximant of
order [ndegree, ddegree].

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_numer_degree
documentation:
Reduce degree of numerator to `degree`.
Warning, this assumes `self.var` is much smaller than 1.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_denom_degree
documentation:
Reduce degree of denominator to `degree`.
Warning, this assumes `self.var` is much smaller than 1.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_degree
documentation:
Reduce degree of numerator and denominator to `degree`.
Warning, this assumes `self.var` is much smaller than 1.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_pade
documentation:
Approximate expression using a Pade series with numerator degree
`ndegree` and denominator degree `ddegree` around
`self.var = var0`.

This determines the Pade coeficients from a Taylor series
expansion and thus fails if the expression has singularities
at the origin.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate_taylor
documentation:
Approximate expression using a Taylor series
around `self.var = var0` to degree `degree`.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.approximate
documentation:
Approximate an expression.  The order of attempted approximations is:
1. Fractional powers
2. Exponential functions
3. Hyperbolic trigonometric functions
4. Dominant terms (if `defs` is defined).

See also `approximate_numer_degree`, `approximate_denom_degree`, and
`approximate_degree` methods.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.as_value_unit
documentation:
Return tuple of value and unit.  For example,

>> > v = voltage(5)
>> > v.as_value_unit
(5, volts)

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.as_N_D
documentation:
Responses due to a sum of delayed transient responses
cannot be factored into ZPK form with a constant delay.
For example, sometimes SymPy gives:

    ⎛    s⋅τ     ⎞ - s⋅τ
    ⎝V₁⋅ℯ   -  V₂⎠⋅ℯ
I = ────────────────────
       s⋅(L⋅s + R)

This method tries to extract the numerator and denominator
where the denominator is a polynomial.

N, D = I.as_N_D()

             -s⋅τ
N = V₁ - V₂⋅ℯ
D = s⋅(L⋅s + R)

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.as_sum
documentation:
Responses due to a sum of delayed transient responses
cannot be factored into ZPK form with a constant delay.
For example, sometimes SymPy gives:

    ⎛    s⋅τ   ⎞  - s⋅τ
    ⎝V₁⋅ℯ - V₂ ⎠⋅ℯ
I = ────────────────────
       s⋅(L⋅s + R)

While this cannot be factored into ZPK form, it can be
expressed as a sum of ZPK forms or as a partial fraction
expansion.  However, SymPy does not play ball if trying to
express as a sum of terms:

I.as_ordered_terms()

⎡⎛    s⋅τ  ⎞  - s⋅τ  ⎤
⎢⎝V₁⋅ℯ - V₂⎠⋅ℯ       ⎥
⎢────────────────────⎥
⎣    s⋅(L⋅s + R)     ⎦

Instead, it appears necessary to split into N / D where
D is a polynomial.  Then N can be split.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.as_monic_terms
documentation:
Rewrite terms so that each denominator is monic.

This does not expand the expression first; use `.expand()`.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.as_nonmonic_terms
documentation:
Rewrite terms so that each denominator is not monic.

This does not expand the expression first; use `.expand()`.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.as_continued_fraction
documentation: Convert expression into a continued fraction.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.continued_fraction_inverse_coeffs
documentation:
Convert expression into a continued fraction with inverse
coefficients.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.estimate
documentation:
Estimate the parameters of an expression that best fits measured data using
non-linear least squares optimization techniques.

`x` is an ndarray of values for the dependent variable,
`y` is an ndarray of values for the independent variable,
`method` is the optimization method (including: 'brute', 'trf' (default), 'dogbox',
'Nelder-Mead\`, `Powell`),
`ranges` is a dictionary of the search ranges keyed by the parameter name,
`Ns` is the number of steps per range for the 'brute' optimizer,
`iterations` is the number of iterations to use for outlier removal.
`positive` sets the minimum parameter value to zero for parameters
not specified in `ranges`
`param_min` sets the minimum parameter value for parameters
not specified in `ranges`
`param_max` sets the maximum parameter value for parameters
not specified in `ranges`

The returned result is a `FitterResult` object. This has attributes:
`params` a dictionary of the estimated parameter values keyed by parameter name,
`rmse` the root mean squared error.

Here's an example of use:
\`
\>>> e = expr('a * exp(-t / tau) * u(t)')
\>>> tv = arange(100)
\>>> vv = e.subs({'a': 1, 'tau': 10}).evaluate(tv)
\>>> results = e.estimate(tv, vv, ranges={'a': (0, 10), 'tau': (1, 20)})
\>>> results.params
{'a': 1.000000000048109, 'tau': 9.999999998432187}
\>>> results.rmse
3.489526384702217e-22
\`

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.remove_undefs
documentation:
Replace applied undefined functions(undefs) with symbols, for
example, replace x(t) with x, etc.

This is useful for simplifying and solving equations.

This method gives up if it finds an expression such as
x(n - 1) + 2 * x(n) since the arguments are different.

If return_mappings is True, then a dictionary of substitutions
is returned as well as the modified expression.  For example,

new, defs = expr.remove_undefs(return_mappings=True)

The original expression can be obtained using:

new.subs(defs)

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.remove_condition
documentation:
Remove the piecewise condition from the expression.
See also force_causal.

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.remove_images
documentation:
Remove all spectral images resulting from a DTFT.

For example,

>> > x = Sum(DiracDelta(f - m/Delta_t), (m, -oo, oo))
>> > x.remove_images()
DiracDelta(f)

Alternatively, the number of images can be changed,
for example,

>> > x = Sum(DiracDelta(f - m/Delta_t), (m, -1, 1))
>> > x.remove_images()
Sum(DiracDelta(f - m/Delta_t), (f, -1, 1))

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.as_QRF
documentation:
Decompose expression into Q, R, F, delay, undef where

expression = (Q + sum_n r_n / f_n) * exp(-delay * var) * undef

`method` can be 'sub' (substitution method, the default) or
'ec' (equating cofficients method).

methods.name: lcapy.expr.Expr.as_QRPO
documentation:
Decompose expression into Q, R, P, O, delay, undef where

`expression = (Q + sum_n r_n / (var - p_n)**o_n) * exp(-delay * var) * undef`

`method` can be 'sub' (substitution method, the default) or
'ec' (equating cofficients method).
Facade class for sympy classes derived from sympy.Expr.
